cases.title: Sepsis (respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 19-year-old man is febrile and looks acutely unwell with a productive cough, rapid breathing, and new confusion. His blood pressure is low and he is becoming increasingly drowsy.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 26.5 mmHg and has moved down while the pH has moved up, so the primary process is respiratory alkalosis. This is the respiratory side of ROME: PaCO2 and pH move in opposite directions, so losing CO2 raises the pH.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits sepsis-related hyperventilation without an added metabolic process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In acute respiratory alkalosis, buffering lowers HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 should fall by 2 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 21.3 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.3-23.3). The measured HCO3 is 21.1 mmol/L. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate metabolic compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Sepsis can increase respiratory drive, causing hyperventilation and a low PaCO₂. This respiratory alkalosis may appear early, before a metabolic acidosis develops. A normal pH can still hide an important acid-base process.

cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 79-year-old man has become increasingly tired and confused over the past few days, with poor appetite and reduced urine output. His family says he has also been nauseated and more drowsy than usual.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.35, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 18 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is most consistent with uraemic HAGMA from renal failure.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 35.0 mmHg (acceptable range 33.0-37.0). The measured PaCO2 is 34.1 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 136 - (98 + 18) = 20.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Uraemia causes a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis because impaired renal excretion allows unmeasured acids to accumulate. Bicarbonate falls as these acids are buffered. Respiratory compensation lowers PaCO₂.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Renal failure can explain a raised anion gap, but the compensation still has to fit before you call it a single process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: A 14-year-old boy is brought in by his anxious mother after she applied oil of wintergreen to his sore knees. He had become sweaty, nauseated, and started breathing fast. He also mentioned a ringing in his ears. This is his blood gas.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the classic respiratory alkalosis plus high anion gap metabolic acidosis pattern of salicylate toxicity

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 24.9 mmHg and has moved down while the pH has moved up, so the primary process is respiratory alkalosis. This is the respiratory side of ROME: PaCO2 and pH move in opposite directions, so losing CO2 raises the pH.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This is the classic salicylate pattern: respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In acute respiratory alkalosis, buffering lowers HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 should fall by 2 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 21.0 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.0-23.0). The measured HCO3 is 13.1 mmol/L. Because the measured HCO3 is below the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 137 - (93 + 13.1) = 30.9, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: No third process is identified. The compensation step suggested a second acid-base process, and the raised anion gap identifies this as HAGMA. Delta gap reasoning does not show an additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis on top of the HAGMA: the bicarbonate level is not clearly lower or higher than expected once the respiratory disorder and raised-gap metabolic acidosis are considered.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Salicylate toxicity classically causes both respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis. The two processes can partially offset each other, so the pH may look deceptively normal. The mixed pattern is the important clue.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Salicylates are sneaky: alkalosis and acidosis can happen at the same time, sometimes with a deceptively normal pH.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 66-year-old man comes in feeling weak and light-headed after several days of frequent loose stools and poor oral intake. He also reports crampy abdominal discomfort and looks dehydrated.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 17 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss leads to a metabolic acidosis, reflected by a low bicarbonate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 33.5 mmHg (acceptable range 31.5-35.5). The measured PaCO2 is 35.3 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 136 - (107 + 17) = 12.0, which is normal. This makes a raised-gap metabolic acidosis less likely.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diarrhoea causes normal anion gap metabolic acidosis through direct bicarbonate loss from the gut. Chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality, creating a hyperchloraemic acidosis. The pattern may occur with volume depletion and low potassium.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A normal anion gap acidosis points away from hidden acids and toward bicarbonate loss — often from the gut.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 43-year-old man comes in feeling weak and lightheaded after his diuretic dose was recently increased. He also reports a dry mouth and needing to stand up slowly because of postural dizziness.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 36 mmol/L and has moved up with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic alkalosis. High HCO3 represents excess base in the blood, so it directly pushes the pH upward.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis. Diuretic use causes volume depletion and chloride loss, which maintains the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic alkalosis, the body compensates by hypoventilating to retain PaCO2. Using the metabolic alkalosis compensation rule, the expected PaCO2 is about 48.4 mmHg (acceptable range 45.4-51.4). The measured PaCO2 is 47.6 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diuretics can cause metabolic alkalosis through volume depletion and renal chloride loss. Chloride depletion helps the kidney retain bicarbonate, which maintains the alkalosis. Potassium is often low or low-normal.

final_diagnosis.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis.

compensation.body: Expected PaCO2 is about 48.4 mmHg; measured PaCO2 is 47.6 mmHg, so compensation is appropriate.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 48-year-old man has become increasingly confused and tachycardic overnight, with cool peripheries and rising oxygen needs. His blood pressure is soft despite fluids, and he is complaining of vague abdominal pain.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 12.9 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactate is 6.3 mmol/L, which supports lactic acidosis in the septic clinical context.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 27.4 mmHg (acceptable range 25.4-29.4). The measured PaCO2 is 28.7 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 140 - (95 + 12.9) = 32.1, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactic acidosis occurs when lactate accumulates from impaired tissue oxygen delivery or cellular stress. This produces a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Common contexts include sepsis, shock, severe anaemia, or ischaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactate may explain the gap, but do not stop there — check whether the rest of the gas still makes sense.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 70-year-old man presents with several days of vomiting and poor oral intake. He now feels weak and dizzy. Here is his blood gas

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 39 mmol/L and has moved up with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic alkalosis. High HCO3 represents excess base in the blood, so it directly pushes the pH upward.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from gastric losses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic alkalosis, the body compensates by hypoventilating to retain PaCO2. Using the metabolic alkalosis compensation rule, the expected PaCO2 is about 50.5 mmHg (acceptable range 47.5-53.5). The measured PaCO2 is 51.8 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Vomiting causes loss of gastric acid, raising bicarbonate and producing metabolic alkalosis. The body compensates by retaining CO₂ through hypoventilation. Ongoing chloride and volume depletion help keep the alkalosis going.

cases.title: Starvation ketosis (subtle HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 30-year-old man presents with several days of poor oral intake after a minor viral illness. He feels weak, nauseated, and has lost some weight, with mild dehydration on examination.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise starvation ketosis as a subtle high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation and normal-range glucose

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 15 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 3.3 mmol/L, which is not markedly elevated. This is more in keeping with starvation ketosis over DKA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 30.5 mmHg (acceptable range 28.5-32.5). The measured PaCO2 is 31.8 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 138 - (99 + 15) = 24.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Poor oral intake can increase ketone production and cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is usually milder than DKA because some insulin activity remains. The key clue is ketosis with normal or only mildly elevated glucose.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Ketones without major hyperglycaemia are not automatically DKA. The story around the gas matters.

ph_status.title: pH Status
ph_status.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 27-year-old woman is brought in by ambulance drowsy, pale, and tachycardic after several hours of vomiting and severe abdominal pain. Her hands are cool, and she looks poorly perfused.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 15.3 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactate is 7.1 mmol/L, which supports lactic acidosis in the septic clinical context.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 31.0 mmHg (acceptable range 29.0-33.0). The measured PaCO2 is 30.3 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 142 - (97 + 15.3) = 29.7, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactic acidosis occurs when lactate accumulates from impaired tissue oxygen delivery or cellular stress. This produces a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Common contexts include sepsis, shock, severe anaemia, or ischaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactate may explain the gap, but do not stop there — check whether the rest of the gas still makes sense.

cases.title: Panic / hyperventilation (acute respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 26 year old male in ED with rapid breathing after a panic episode, complaining of tingling in both hands and feeling light-headed. He is anxious and speaking in short phrases.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 24 mmHg and has moved down while the pH has moved up, so the primary process is respiratory alkalosis. This is the respiratory side of ROME: PaCO2 and pH move in opposite directions, so losing CO2 raises the pH.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits acute hyperventilation such as panic.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Hyperventilation increases CO₂ clearance, causing PaCO₂ to fall and pH to rise. Because this is usually acute, bicarbonate has little time to change. The pattern is respiratory alkalosis driven by increased breathing rather than a primary metabolic process.

cases.title: Toxic alcohol (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 30-year-old man is brought in after drinking homemade liquor at a friend’s party, and now has blurred vision, vomiting, and a worsening headache. He seems confused and unsure exactly how much he drank.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise toxic alcohol ingestion as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 12 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The clinical context and only modest lactate of 1.9 mmol/L support toxic alcohol ingestion over isolated lactic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 26.0 mmHg (acceptable range 24.0-28.0). The measured PaCO2 is 27.3 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 137 - (104 + 12) = 21.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Toxic alcohol ingestion can produce a severe raised anion gap metabolic acidosis as acidic metabolites accumulate. The acidosis may be out of proportion to the measured lactate. The key pattern is an unexplained high anion gap acidosis without another clear cause.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A severe unexplained high anion gap acidosis, especially with lactate that does not explain it, should make toxic alcohols ring alarm bells.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 74-year-old woman presents with cramps, thirst, and mild light-headedness after recent escalation of diuretic therapy. She has been eating poorly and appears mildly dehydrated.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 35 mmol/L and has moved up with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic alkalosis. High HCO3 represents excess base in the blood, so it directly pushes the pH upward.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis. Diuretic use causes volume depletion and chloride loss, which maintains the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic alkalosis, the body compensates by hypoventilating to retain PaCO2. Using the metabolic alkalosis compensation rule, the expected PaCO2 is about 47.7 mmHg (acceptable range 44.7-50.7). The measured PaCO2 is 47.0 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 134 - (87 + 35) = 12.0, which is normal. This makes a raised-gap metabolic acidosis less likely.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diuretics can cause metabolic alkalosis through volume depletion and renal chloride loss. Chloride depletion helps the kidney retain bicarbonate, which maintains the alkalosis. Potassium is often low or low-normal.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Diuretics can leave the body chloride- and volume-depleted, which helps keep the alkalosis going.

cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)
cases.clinical_stem: A 29-year-old woman is brought in after two days of vomiting and poor oral intake following a flu-like illness. She has missed some insulin doses and is now complaining of abdominal discomfort, thirst, and rapid breathing.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This fits DKA with a concurrent metabolic alkalosis from vomiting rather than an isolated ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 27.5 mmHg (acceptable range 25.5-29.5). The measured PaCO2 is 27.6 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 139 - (93 + 13) = 33.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Delta gap reasoning compares the rise in anion gap with the fall in HCO3. With an anion gap of 33.0, a pure raised-gap metabolic acidosis would usually lower HCO3 to about 3.0 mmol/L. The measured HCO3 is 13 mmol/L, which is higher than expected. This means the bicarbonate has been relatively preserved, indicating an additional metabolic alkalosis. Vomiting is the likely source of the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: DKA causes a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis from ketone production. Vomiting can add a metabolic alkalosis, making the bicarbonate higher than expected. This can make the acidosis look less severe than it really is.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: In DKA, a not-too-low bicarbonate is not always reassuring. Vomiting can hide how bad the acidosis really is.

cases.title: Respiratory acidosis with concurrent HAGMA
cases.clinical_stem: A 71-year-old woman with chronic lung disease is brought in with worsening breathlessness, increasing drowsiness, and poor oral intake over several days. She is febrile, looks unwell, and her family report a productive cough and reduced responsiveness today.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory acidosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 67.7 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis. CO2 behaves like an acid load: as PaCO2 rises, more carbonic acid is generated and the pH falls.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory acidosis with concurrent HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by 4 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 35.1 mmol/L (acceptable range 33.1-37.1). The measured HCO3 is 27.1 mmol/L. Because the measured HCO3 is below the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 142 - (95 + 27.1) = 19.9, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: No third process is identified. The compensation step suggested a second acid-base process, and the raised anion gap identifies this as HAGMA. Delta gap reasoning does not show an additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis on top of the HAGMA: the bicarbonate level is not clearly lower or higher than expected once the respiratory disorder and raised-gap metabolic acidosis are considered.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern combines impaired ventilation with a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. PaCO₂ retention pushes the pH down, while the metabolic acidosis adds a second acidifying process. The result is often a more severe acidaemia than either process alone.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high PaCO₂ plus a raised anion gap means two problems may be happening at once.

cases.title: Sepsis (respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 55-year-old man presents with fever, pleuritic chest discomfort, productive cough, and worsening shortness of breath. He is hypotensive, tachycardic, and breathing rapidly on arrival.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 22.8 mmHg and has moved down while the pH has moved up, so the primary process is respiratory alkalosis. This is the respiratory side of ROME: PaCO2 and pH move in opposite directions, so losing CO2 raises the pH.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits sepsis-related hyperventilation without an added metabolic process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In acute respiratory alkalosis, buffering lowers HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 should fall by 2 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 20.6 mmol/L (acceptable range 18.6-22.6). The measured HCO3 is 20.0 mmol/L. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate metabolic compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 137 - (105 + 20.0) = 12.0, which is normal. This makes a raised-gap metabolic acidosis less likely.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Sepsis can increase respiratory drive, causing hyperventilation and a low PaCO₂. This respiratory alkalosis may appear early, before a metabolic acidosis develops. A normal pH can still hide an important acid-base process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A low PaCO₂ in a sick patient can be an early warning sign. A normal pH does not mean a normal gas.

The anion gap is 136 - (98 + 15) = 23.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins. The overall pattern is most consistent with DKA with inadequate respiratory compensation.

cases.title: Alcoholic ketoacidosis (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 46-year-old woman with heavy alcohol use comes in shaking, nauseated, and unable to keep food down after several days of poor intake. She has been vomiting and feels increasingly weak and dehydrated.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise alcoholic ketoacidosis as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 7 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The history pattern and the HAGMA fit alcoholic ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 18.5 mmHg (acceptable range 16.5-20.5). The measured PaCO2 is 17.8 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 129 - (87 + 7) = 35.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Alcoholic ketoacidosis occurs when heavy alcohol use and poor intake drive ketone production. Glucose is often normal or low, which helps separate it from DKA. Vomiting may add a metabolic alkalosis and partially mask the acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis without a very high glucose should make you think beyond DKA — alcoholic ketoacidosis is one of the big ones.

cases.title: Mixed HAGMA and metabolic alkalosis
cases.clinical_stem: A 25-year-old woman comes in with several days of persistent vomiting, poor oral intake, and worsening abdominal discomfort after a flu-like illness. She looks dehydrated and says she has barely been able to keep fluids down.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise a mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent metabolic alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.35, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 22 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is a mixed HAGMA with concurrent metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 41.0 mmHg (acceptable range 39.0-43.0). The measured PaCO2 is 41.2 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 142 - (94 + 22) = 26.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Delta gap reasoning compares the rise in anion gap with the fall in HCO3. With an anion gap of 26.0, a pure raised-gap metabolic acidosis would usually lower HCO3 to about 10.0 mmol/L. The measured HCO3 is 22 mmol/L, which is higher than expected. This means the bicarbonate has been relatively preserved, indicating an additional metabolic alkalosis. This pattern can occur when acid generation is combined with vomiting or diuretic effect.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern combines acid generation with a process that raises bicarbonate, such as vomiting or diuretic effect. The anion gap reveals the metabolic acidosis, while the higher-than-expected bicarbonate reveals the alkalosis. The key is recognising that both processes can be present at once.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap shows the acidosis; the bicarbonate tells you something is pushing back the other way.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 57-year-old man is referred after a recent increase in his diuretic dose. He has felt weak, light-headed on standing, and has a very dry mouth.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 36 mmol/L and has moved up with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic alkalosis. High HCO3 represents excess base in the blood, so it directly pushes the pH upward.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis. Diuretic use causes volume depletion and chloride loss, which maintains the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic alkalosis, the body compensates by hypoventilating to retain PaCO2. Using the metabolic alkalosis compensation rule, the expected PaCO2 is about 48.4 mmHg (acceptable range 45.4-51.4). The measured PaCO2 is 49.8 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diuretics can cause metabolic alkalosis through volume depletion and renal chloride loss. Chloride depletion helps the kidney retain bicarbonate, which maintains the alkalosis. Potassium is often low or low-normal.

cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)
cases.clinical_stem: A 40-year-old woman is brought in after several days of vomiting and poor intake following a flu-like illness, and she has missed several insulin doses. She looks dehydrated and is breathing deeply and rapidly, with mild abdominal discomfort.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This fits DKA with a concurrent metabolic alkalosis from vomiting rather than an isolated ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 27.5 mmHg (acceptable range 25.5-29.5). The measured PaCO2 is 27.7 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 139 - (96 + 13) = 30.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Delta gap reasoning compares the rise in anion gap with the fall in HCO3. With an anion gap of 30.0, a pure raised-gap metabolic acidosis would usually lower HCO3 to about 6.0 mmol/L. The measured HCO3 is 13 mmol/L, which is higher than expected. This means the bicarbonate has been relatively preserved, indicating an additional metabolic alkalosis. Vomiting is the likely source of the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: DKA causes a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis from ketone production. Vomiting can add a metabolic alkalosis, making the bicarbonate higher than expected. This can make the acidosis look less severe than it really is.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: In DKA, a not-too-low bicarbonate is not always reassuring. Vomiting can hide how bad the acidosis really is.

cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 61-year-old woman has become progressively drowsy and nauseated over the last day, with poor oral intake and noticeably reduced urine output. She is weak, confused, and looks fluid overloaded on examination.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder

questions_flow.label: Anion gap

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 14 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is most consistent with uraemic HAGMA from renal failure.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 29.0 mmHg (acceptable range 27.0-31.0). The measured PaCO2 is 29.1 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 137 - (101 + 14) = 22.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Uraemia causes a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis because impaired renal excretion allows unmeasured acids to accumulate. Bicarbonate falls as these acids are buffered. Respiratory compensation lowers PaCO₂.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Renal failure can explain a raised anion gap, but the compensation still has to fit before you call it a single process.

cases.title: Respiratory acidosis with concurrent HAGMA
cases.clinical_stem: A 43-year-old man is becoming increasingly drowsy and breathless, with shallow respirations and a feverish, unwell appearance. He has been more confused since earlier today and is now harder to rouse.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory acidosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 65.5 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis. CO2 behaves like an acid load: as PaCO2 rises, more carbonic acid is generated and the pH falls.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory acidosis with concurrent HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by 4 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 34.2 mmol/L (acceptable range 32.2-36.2). The measured HCO3 is 31.8 mmol/L. Because the measured HCO3 is below the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 137 - (86 + 31.8) = 19.2, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: No third process is identified. The compensation step suggested a second acid-base process, and the raised anion gap identifies this as HAGMA. Delta gap reasoning does not show an additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis on top of the HAGMA: the bicarbonate level is not clearly lower or higher than expected once the respiratory disorder and raised-gap metabolic acidosis are considered.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern combines impaired ventilation with a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. PaCO₂ retention pushes the pH down, while the metabolic acidosis adds a second acidifying process. The result is often a more severe acidaemia than either process alone.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high PaCO₂ plus a raised anion gap means two problems may be happening at once.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from gastric losses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Metabolic alkalosis occurs when bicarbonate is relatively increased, often from hydrogen ion loss or volume contraction. Chloride is often low because chloride depletion helps maintain the alkalosis. Gastric acid loss from vomiting is a common example.

cases.title: Sepsis (respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 50-year-old woman presents with spreading leg erythema, fever, rigors, and generalised weakness. She is anxious, breathing quickly, and looks systemically unwell.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 27.9 mmHg and has moved down while the pH has moved up, so the primary process is respiratory alkalosis. This is the respiratory side of ROME: PaCO2 and pH move in opposite directions, so losing CO2 raises the pH.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits sepsis-related hyperventilation without an added metabolic process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In acute respiratory alkalosis, buffering lowers HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 should fall by 2 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 21.6 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.6-23.6). The measured HCO3 is 21.0 mmol/L. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate metabolic compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 140 - (106 + 21.0) = 13.0, which is normal. This makes a raised-gap metabolic acidosis less likely.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Sepsis can increase respiratory drive, causing hyperventilation and a low PaCO₂. This respiratory alkalosis may appear early, before a metabolic acidosis develops. A normal pH can still hide an important acid-base process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A low PaCO₂ in a sick patient can be an early warning sign. A normal pH does not mean a normal gas.

cases.title: Alcoholic ketoacidosis (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 17 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The history pattern and the HAGMA fit alcoholic ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 33.5 mmHg (acceptable range 31.5-35.5). The measured PaCO2 is 32.5 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 136 - (98 + 17) = 21.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Alcoholic ketoacidosis occurs when heavy alcohol use and poor intake drive ketone production. Glucose is often normal or low, which helps separate it from DKA. Vomiting may add a metabolic alkalosis and partially mask the acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis without a very high glucose should make you think beyond DKA — alcoholic ketoacidosis is one of the big ones.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 76-year-old woman on high-dose diuretics presents with worsening postural symptoms, muscle weakness, and poor appetite. Her dose was recently increased after worsening ankle swelling.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 35 mmol/L and has moved up with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic alkalosis. High HCO3 represents excess base in the blood, so it directly pushes the pH upward.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis. Diuretic use causes volume depletion and chloride loss, which maintains the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic alkalosis, the body compensates by hypoventilating to retain PaCO2. Using the metabolic alkalosis compensation rule, the expected PaCO2 is about 47.7 mmHg (acceptable range 44.7-50.7). The measured PaCO2 is 48.4 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 139 - (92 + 35) = 12.0, which is normal. This makes a raised-gap metabolic acidosis less likely.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diuretics can cause metabolic alkalosis through volume depletion and renal chloride loss. Chloride depletion helps the kidney retain bicarbonate, which maintains the alkalosis. Potassium is often low or low-normal.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Diuretics can leave the body chloride- and volume-depleted, which helps keep the alkalosis going.

cases.title: Toxic alcohol (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 30-year-old woman is brought in after telling family she had swallowed antifreeze in a suicide attempt. She is nauseated, confused, and complaining that her vision has become blurry, but the exact amount and timing are unclear.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise toxic alcohol ingestion as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 8 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: In the appropriate clinical context, a HAGMA should prompt consideration of toxic alcohol ingestion.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 20.0 mmHg (acceptable range 18.0-22.0). explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 139 - (93 + 8) = 38.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Toxic alcohol ingestion can produce a severe raised anion gap metabolic acidosis as acidic metabolites accumulate. The acidosis may be out of proportion to the measured lactate. The key pattern is an unexplained high anion gap acidosis without another clear cause.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A severe unexplained high anion gap acidosis, especially with lactate that does not explain it, should make toxic alcohols ring alarm bells.

cases.title: Opioid toxicity (acute respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: An 82 year old female is day 1 post total hip replacement. She is drowsy, with a respiratory rate of 6/min and pinpoint pupils. A blood gas is taken due to concern about hypoventilation.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis due to hypoventilation with appropriate compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 73 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis. CO2 behaves like an acid load: as PaCO2 rises, more carbonic acid is generated and the pH falls.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits opioid-related hypoventilation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Opioid toxicity causes respiratory depression, reducing ventilation and allowing CO₂ to build up. The result is respiratory acidosis with a high PaCO₂ and low pH. The gas pattern reflects inadequate ventilation rather than a primary metabolic problem.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)

cases.title: Panic / hyperventilation (acute respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 28 year old male presents with acute severe flank pain from suspected renal colic. He is restless at the bedside and is in significant pain. A blood gas was taken.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 24 mmHg and has moved down while the pH has moved up, so the primary process is respiratory alkalosis. This is the respiratory side of ROME: PaCO2 and pH move in opposite directions, so losing CO2 raises the pH.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits acute hyperventilation such as panic.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Hyperventilation increases CO₂ clearance, causing PaCO₂ to fall and pH to rise. Because this is usually acute, bicarbonate has little time to change. The pattern is respiratory alkalosis driven by increased breathing rather than a primary metabolic process.

cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)
cases.clinical_stem: A 20-year-old woman is brought in after several hours of repeated vomiting and worsening thirst. She admits she has missed her insulin and is now struggling to keep fluids down, with increasing abdominal discomfort and fast, deep breathing.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This fits DKA with a concurrent metabolic alkalosis from vomiting rather than an isolated ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 27.5 mmHg (acceptable range 25.5-29.5). The measured PaCO2 is 28.2 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 136 - (95 + 13) = 28.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Delta gap reasoning compares the rise in anion gap with the fall in HCO3. With an anion gap of 28.0, a pure raised-gap metabolic acidosis would usually lower HCO3 to about 8.0 mmol/L. The measured HCO3 is 13 mmol/L, which is higher than expected. This means the bicarbonate has been relatively preserved, indicating an additional metabolic alkalosis. Vomiting is the likely source of the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: DKA causes a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis from ketone production. Vomiting can add a metabolic alkalosis, making the bicarbonate higher than expected. This can make the acidosis look less severe than it really is.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: In DKA, a not-too-low bicarbonate is not always reassuring. Vomiting can hide how bad the acidosis really is.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 67-year-old woman with long-standing breathlessness and a heavy smoking history comes in more short of breath than usual. She has a productive cough, audible wheeze, and seems a little sleepy but is still answering questions.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 63 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis. CO2 behaves like an acid load: as PaCO2 rises, more carbonic acid is generated and the pH falls.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chronic CO2 retention in the setting of COPD.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by 4 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 33.2 mmol/L (acceptable range 31.2-35.2). The measured HCO3 is 32.9 mmol/L. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate metabolic compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Chronic CO₂ retention in COPD leads to a persistently high PaCO₂. Over time, the kidneys retain bicarbonate, bringing the pH closer to normal. This creates a compensated respiratory acidosis pattern.

primary_disorder.title: Primary Disorder

final_diagnosis.title: Diagnosis
final_diagnosis.body: This pattern fits chronic CO2 retention in the setting of COPD.

compensation.title: Compensation
compensation.body: Expected HCO3 is about 33.2 mmol/L; measured HCO3 is 32.9 mmol/L, so compensation is appropriate.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 61-year-old woman with known COPD on home oxygen is brought in after becoming drowsy during a respiratory illness. She has increased work of breathing.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 85.0 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis. CO2 behaves like an acid load: as PaCO2 rises, more carbonic acid is generated and the pH falls.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is consistent with an acute on chronic respiratory acidosis. Chronic CO2 retention leads to metabolic compensation, with an acute rise in the PaCO2 during exacerbation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by 4 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 42.0 mmol/L (acceptable range 40.0-44.0). The measured HCO3 is 38.7 mmol/L. The acute-only expected HCO3 is about 28.5 mmol/L (range 26.5-30.5). The measured HCO3 sits between the acute and stable chronic expected responses, supporting acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis without implying a precise combined target.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 139 - (102 + 38.7) = -1.7, which is normal. This makes a raised-gap metabolic acidosis less likely.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: An acute COPD exacerbation can worsen ventilation, causing PaCO₂ to rise further and pH to fall. The bicarbonate may already be elevated from chronic compensation, but it has not had time to fully match the acute change. This creates an acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis pattern.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: If the PaCO₂ has risen but the bicarbonate has not caught up, think acute-on-chronic respiratory failure.

cases.title: Respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA
cases.clinical_stem: A 73-year-old man has been increasingly unwell since yesterday with fast breathing, nausea, and a vague central abdominal ache. His family say he seems more confused and has had a low-grade fever and poor oral intake.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory alkalosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.35, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 26.6 mmHg and has moved down while the pH has moved up, so the primary process is respiratory alkalosis. This is the respiratory side of ROME: PaCO2 and pH move in opposite directions, so losing CO2 raises the pH.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In acute respiratory alkalosis, buffering lowers HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 should fall by 2 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 21.3 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.3-23.3). The measured HCO3 is 14.1 mmol/L. Because the measured HCO3 is below the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 141 - (98 + 14.1) = 28.9, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: No third process is identified. The compensation step suggested a second acid-base process, and the raised anion gap identifies this as HAGMA. Delta gap reasoning does not show an additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis on top of the HAGMA: the bicarbonate level is not clearly lower or higher than expected once the respiratory disorder and raised-gap metabolic acidosis are considered.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern combines hyperventilation with a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. The respiratory alkalosis can lift the pH toward normal, masking the metabolic acidosis. Sepsis is one context where this mixed pattern can appear.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A very low PaCO₂ does not explain everything. Check the anion gap for the hidden metabolic acidosis.

cases.title: Sepsis (respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 63-year-old man is brought in feeling unwell with a fever, productive cough, and increasing shortness of breath. He looks clammy and confused, with rapid breathing and low blood pressure.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 23.4 mmHg and has moved down while the pH has moved up, so the primary process is respiratory alkalosis. This is the respiratory side of ROME: PaCO2 and pH move in opposite directions, so losing CO2 raises the pH.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits sepsis-related hyperventilation without an added metabolic process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In acute respiratory alkalosis, buffering lowers HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 should fall by 2 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 20.7 mmol/L (acceptable range 18.7-22.7). The measured HCO3 is 20.9 mmol/L. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate metabolic compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Sepsis can increase respiratory drive, causing hyperventilation and a low PaCO₂. This respiratory alkalosis may appear early, before a metabolic acidosis develops. A normal pH can still hide an important acid-base process.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 72-year-old woman is brought in feeling weak and light-headed after several days of frequent loose stools and poor oral intake. She also reports crampy lower abdominal discomfort and reduced urine output.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss leads to a metabolic acidosis, reflected by a low bicarbonate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 27.5 mmHg (acceptable range 25.5-29.5). The measured PaCO2 is 27.1 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 137 - (112 + 13) = 12.0, which is normal. This makes a raised-gap metabolic acidosis less likely.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diarrhoea causes normal anion gap metabolic acidosis through direct bicarbonate loss from the gut. Chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality, creating a hyperchloraemic acidosis. The pattern may occur with volume depletion and low potassium.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A normal anion gap acidosis points away from hidden acids and toward bicarbonate loss — often from the gut.

cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)
cases.clinical_stem: A 21-year-old woman is brought in after several hours of repeated vomiting and diffuse abdominal pain, with her family noting she has been drinking lots of water and passing urine more than usual over the past few days. She appears dehydrated and is breathing rapidly.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This fits DKA with a concurrent metabolic alkalosis from vomiting rather than an isolated ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 27.5 mmHg (acceptable range 25.5-29.5). The measured PaCO2 is 27.5 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 135 - (91 + 13) = 31.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Delta gap reasoning compares the rise in anion gap with the fall in HCO3. With an anion gap of 31.0, a pure raised-gap metabolic acidosis would usually lower HCO3 to about 5.0 mmol/L. The measured HCO3 is 13 mmol/L, which is higher than expected. This means the bicarbonate has been relatively preserved, indicating an additional metabolic alkalosis. Vomiting is the likely source of the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: DKA causes a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis from ketone production. Vomiting can add a metabolic alkalosis, making the bicarbonate higher than expected. This can make the acidosis look less severe than it really is.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: In DKA, a not-too-low bicarbonate is not always reassuring. Vomiting can hide how bad the acidosis really is.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder

explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chronic CO2 retention in the setting of COPD.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by 4 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 33.6 mmol/L (acceptable range 31.6-35.6). explanation_blueprint.body: Chronic CO₂ retention in COPD leads to a persistently high PaCO₂. Over time, the kidneys retain bicarbonate, bringing the pH closer to normal. This creates a compensated respiratory acidosis pattern.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high PaCO₂ with a near-normal pH often means the kidneys have had time to adapt. Compare with baseline before calling it acute.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 140 - (96 + 24) = 20.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Delta gap reasoning compares the rise in anion gap with the fall in HCO3. With an anion gap of 20.0, a pure raised-gap metabolic acidosis would usually lower HCO3 to about 16.0 mmol/L. The measured HCO3 is 24 mmol/L, which is higher than expected. This means the bicarbonate has been relatively preserved, indicating an additional metabolic alkalosis. This pattern can occur when acid generation is combined with vomiting or diuretic effect.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern combines acid generation with a process that raises bicarbonate, such as vomiting or diuretic effect. The anion gap reveals the metabolic acidosis, while the higher-than-expected bicarbonate reveals the alkalosis. The key is recognising that both processes can be present at once.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap shows the acidosis; the bicarbonate tells you something is pushing back the other way.
