cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 21-year-old woman is brought in after several days of thirst, passing urine frequently, and increasing fatigue, with new abdominal discomfort and deep breathing today. Her friend says she has been losing weight over the past few weeks and has never had diabetes before.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 16 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 20.5 mmol/L, which strongly supports DKA as the cause of this HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 32.0 mmHg (acceptable range 30.0-34.0). The measured PaCO2 is 32.0 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 142 - (104 + 16) = 22.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: DKA is driven by insulin deficiency, which leads to ketone production and a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Marked hyperglycaemia helps distinguish it from other ketoacidoses. Dehydration and electrolyte shifts often accompany the acid-base disturbance.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: DKA usually gives you a high anion gap acidosis with high glucose — but still check whether the CO₂ and bicarbonate behave the way they should.

cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)
cases.clinical_stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with repeated vomiting and crampy abdominal pain after several days of thirst and weight loss. She looks dehydrated and is breathing fast and deeply.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 12 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This fits DKA with a concurrent metabolic alkalosis from vomiting rather than an isolated ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 26.0 mmHg (acceptable range 24.0-28.0). The measured PaCO2 is 26.8 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 135 - (93 + 12) = 30.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Delta gap reasoning compares the rise in anion gap with the fall in HCO3. With an anion gap of 30.0, a pure raised-gap metabolic acidosis would usually lower HCO3 to about 6.0 mmol/L. The measured HCO3 is 12 mmol/L, which is higher than expected. This means the bicarbonate has been relatively preserved, indicating an additional metabolic alkalosis. Vomiting is the likely source of the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: DKA causes a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis from ketone production. Vomiting can add a metabolic alkalosis, making the bicarbonate higher than expected. This can make the acidosis look less severe than it really is.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: In DKA, a not-too-low bicarbonate is not always reassuring. Vomiting can hide how bad the acidosis really is.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 68-year-old woman with severe chronic lung disease is sent in by her GP after gradually worsening breathlessness and daytime somnolence. She has no chest pain or fever, but her exercise tolerance has declined significantly.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 72 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis. CO2 behaves like an acid load: as PaCO2 rises, more carbonic acid is generated and the pH falls.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chronic CO2 retention in the setting of COPD.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by 4 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 36.8 mmol/L (acceptable range 34.8-38.8). The measured HCO3 is 35.9 mmol/L. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate metabolic compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 137 - (90 + 35.9) = 11.1, which is normal. This makes a raised-gap metabolic acidosis less likely.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Chronic CO₂ retention in COPD leads to a persistently high PaCO₂. Over time, the kidneys retain bicarbonate, bringing the pH closer to normal. This creates a compensated respiratory acidosis pattern.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high PaCO₂ with a near-normal pH often means the kidneys have had time to adapt. Compare with baseline before calling it acute.

cases.title: Toxic alcohol (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 42-year-old woman is brought in after telling family she had swallowed an unknown bottle of screenwash following a fight. She is confused, complaining of abdominal pain and blurred vision, and her breath smells faintly chemical.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise toxic alcohol ingestion as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 5 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: In the appropriate clinical context, a HAGMA should prompt consideration of toxic alcohol ingestion.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 15.5 mmHg (acceptable range 13.5-17.5). The measured PaCO2 is 15.0 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 136 - (93 + 5) = 38.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Toxic alcohol ingestion can produce a severe raised anion gap metabolic acidosis as acidic metabolites accumulate. The acidosis may be out of proportion to the measured lactate. The key pattern is an unexplained high anion gap acidosis without another clear cause.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A severe unexplained high anion gap acidosis, especially with lactate that does not explain it, should make toxic alcohols ring alarm bells.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 71-year-old woman taking loop diuretics for heart failure presents with fatigue, dizziness, and reduced oral intake after several days of increased urine output. She appears volume depleted on examination.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 32 mmol/L and has moved up with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic alkalosis. High HCO3 represents excess base in the blood, so it directly pushes the pH upward.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis. Diuretic use causes volume depletion and chloride loss, which maintains the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic alkalosis, the body compensates by hypoventilating to retain PaCO2. Using the metabolic alkalosis compensation rule, the expected PaCO2 is about 45.6 mmHg (acceptable range 42.6-48.6). The measured PaCO2 is 45.7 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 136 - (94 + 32) = 10.0, which is normal. This makes a raised-gap metabolic acidosis less likely.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diuretics can cause metabolic alkalosis through volume depletion and renal chloride loss. Chloride depletion helps the kidney retain bicarbonate, which maintains the alkalosis. Potassium is often low or low-normal.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Diuretics can leave the body chloride- and volume-depleted, which helps keep the alkalosis going.

cases.title: Sepsis (respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 37-year-old man is brought in with fever, a productive cough, and increasing breathlessness; he looks pale, confused, and tachypnoeic. His blood pressure is low and he is becoming harder to rouse.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 24.5 mmHg and has moved down while the pH has moved up, so the primary process is respiratory alkalosis. This is the respiratory side of ROME: PaCO2 and pH move in opposite directions, so losing CO2 raises the pH.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits sepsis-related hyperventilation without an added metabolic process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In acute respiratory alkalosis, buffering lowers HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 should fall by 2 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 20.9 mmol/L (acceptable range 18.9-22.9). The measured HCO3 is 20.9 mmol/L. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate metabolic compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Sepsis can increase respiratory drive, causing hyperventilation and a low PaCO₂. This respiratory alkalosis may appear early, before a metabolic acidosis develops. A normal pH can still hide an important acid-base process.

explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 63 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis. CO2 behaves like an acid load: as PaCO2 rises, more carbonic acid is generated and the pH falls.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chronic CO2 retention in the setting of COPD.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by 4 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 33.2 mmol/L (acceptable range 31.2-35.2). The measured HCO3 is 33.1 mmol/L. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate metabolic compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 141 - (99 + 33.1) = 8.9, which is normal. This makes a raised-gap metabolic acidosis less likely.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Chronic CO₂ retention in COPD leads to a persistently high PaCO₂. Over time, the kidneys retain bicarbonate, bringing the pH closer to normal. This creates a compensated respiratory acidosis pattern.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high PaCO₂ with a near-normal pH often means the kidneys have had time to adapt. Compare with baseline before calling it acute.

cases.title: Sepsis (respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 60-year-old man is brought to ED with fever, rigors, productive cough, and increasing respiratory distress. He is hypotensive, tachycardic, and unable to speak more than a few words at a time.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 22.6 mmHg and has moved down while the pH has moved up, so the primary process is respiratory alkalosis. This is the respiratory side of ROME: PaCO2 and pH move in opposite directions, so losing CO2 raises the pH.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits sepsis-related hyperventilation without an added metabolic process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In acute respiratory alkalosis, buffering lowers HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 should fall by 2 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 20.5 mmol/L (acceptable range 18.5-22.5). The measured HCO3 is 21.0 mmol/L. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate metabolic compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 136 - (103 + 21.0) = 12.0, which is normal. This makes a raised-gap metabolic acidosis less likely.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Sepsis can increase respiratory drive, causing hyperventilation and a low PaCO₂. This respiratory alkalosis may appear early, before a metabolic acidosis develops. A normal pH can still hide an important acid-base process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A low PaCO₂ in a sick patient can be an early warning sign. A normal pH does not mean a normal gas.

cases.title: Respiratory acidosis with concurrent HAGMA
cases.clinical_stem: A 74 year old man is found increasingly drowsy and breathless on the ward, with shallow respirations and a strong smell of ketones on his breath. He has looked unwell since an episode of poor intake and vomiting overnight.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory acidosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 67.1 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis. CO2 behaves like an acid load: as PaCO2 rises, more carbonic acid is generated and the pH falls.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory acidosis with concurrent HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In acute respiratory acidosis, buffering and early renal compensation raise HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for acute respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by 1 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 26.7 mmol/L (acceptable range 24.7-28.7). The measured HCO3 is 21.9 mmol/L. Because the measured HCO3 is below the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 139 - (92 + 21.9) = 25.1, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: No third process is identified. The compensation step suggested a second acid-base process, and the raised anion gap identifies this as HAGMA. Delta gap reasoning does not show an additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis on top of the HAGMA: the bicarbonate level is not clearly lower or higher than expected once the respiratory disorder and raised-gap metabolic acidosis are considered.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern combines impaired ventilation with a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. PaCO₂ retention pushes the pH down, while the metabolic acidosis adds a second acidifying process. The result is often a more severe acidaemia than either process alone.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high PaCO₂ plus a raised anion gap means two problems may be happening at once.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: A 12-year-old boy is brought in by his anxious mother after she applied a home remedy containing oil of wintergreen to his skin for a sore muscle. He has been vomiting, sweating, and complaining of ringing in his ears.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the classic respiratory alkalosis plus high anion gap metabolic acidosis pattern of salicylate toxicity

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.4, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 25.9 mmHg and has moved down while the pH has moved up, so the primary process is respiratory alkalosis. This is the respiratory side of ROME: PaCO2 and pH move in opposite directions, so losing CO2 raises the pH.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This is the classic salicylate pattern: respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In acute respiratory alkalosis, buffering lowers HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 should fall by 2 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 21.2 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.2-23.2). The measured HCO3 is 15.4 mmol/L. Because the measured HCO3 is below the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 140 - (101 + 15.4) = 23.6, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: No third process is identified. The compensation step suggested a second acid-base process, and the raised anion gap identifies this as HAGMA. Delta gap reasoning does not show an additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis on top of the HAGMA: the bicarbonate level is not clearly lower or higher than expected once the respiratory disorder and raised-gap metabolic acidosis are considered.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Salicylate toxicity classically causes both respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis. The two processes can partially offset each other, so the pH may look deceptively normal. The mixed pattern is the important clue.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Salicylates are sneaky: alkalosis and acidosis can happen at the same time, sometimes with a deceptively normal pH.

cases.title: Mixed HAGMA and metabolic alkalosis
cases.clinical_stem: A 35-year-old man comes in after several days of vomiting and poor oral intake, with worsening epigastric pain and dizziness. He looks dehydrated and says he has barely kept anything down since a recent flu-like illness.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise a mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent metabolic alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.35, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 23 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is a mixed HAGMA with concurrent metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 42.5 mmHg (acceptable range 40.5-44.5). The measured PaCO2 is 42.7 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 138 - (90 + 23) = 25.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Delta gap reasoning compares the rise in anion gap with the fall in HCO3. With an anion gap of 25.0, a pure raised-gap metabolic acidosis would usually lower HCO3 to about 11.0 mmol/L. The measured HCO3 is 23 mmol/L, which is higher than expected. This means the bicarbonate has been relatively preserved, indicating an additional metabolic alkalosis. This pattern can occur when acid generation is combined with vomiting or diuretic effect.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern combines acid generation with a process that raises bicarbonate, such as vomiting or diuretic effect. The anion gap reveals the metabolic acidosis, while the higher-than-expected bicarbonate reveals the alkalosis. The key is recognising that both processes can be present at once.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap shows the acidosis; the bicarbonate tells you something is pushing back the other way.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 77-year-old woman with COPD is brought in by her family because she seems unusually tired. She has had worsening breathlessness, a productive cough, and audible wheeze over the past day.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 88.0 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis. CO2 behaves like an acid load: as PaCO2 rises, more carbonic acid is generated and the pH falls.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is consistent with an acute on chronic respiratory acidosis. Chronic CO2 retention leads to metabolic compensation, with an acute rise in the PaCO2 during exacerbation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by 4 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 43.2 mmol/L (acceptable range 41.2-45.2). The measured HCO3 is 39.0 mmol/L. The acute-only expected HCO3 is about 28.8 mmol/L (range 26.8-30.8). The measured HCO3 sits between the acute and stable chronic expected responses, supporting acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis without implying a precise combined target.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: An acute COPD exacerbation can worsen ventilation, causing PaCO₂ to rise further and pH to fall. The bicarbonate may already be elevated from chronic compensation, but it has not had time to fully match the acute change. This creates an acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis pattern.

cases.title: Alcoholic ketoacidosis (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 51-year-old man is brought in after several days of heavy drinking, then stopping abruptly. He has repeated vomiting, poor oral intake, and looks dehydrated with a fine tremor.

questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise alcoholic ketoacidosis as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 8 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The history pattern and the HAGMA fit alcoholic ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 20.0 mmHg (acceptable range 18.0-22.0). The measured PaCO2 is 21.5 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 131 - (93 + 8) = 30.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Alcoholic ketoacidosis occurs when heavy alcohol use and poor intake drive ketone production. Glucose is often normal or low, which helps separate it from DKA. Vomiting may add a metabolic alkalosis and partially mask the acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis without a very high glucose should make you think beyond DKA — alcoholic ketoacidosis is one of the big ones.

Delta gap reasoning does not show an additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis on top of the HAGMA: the bicarbonate level is not clearly lower or higher than expected once the respiratory disorder and raised-gap metabolic acidosis are considered.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern combines hyperventilation with a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. The respiratory alkalosis can lift the pH toward normal, masking the metabolic acidosis. Sepsis is one context where this mixed pattern can appear.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A very low PaCO₂ does not explain everything. Check the anion gap for the hidden metabolic acidosis.

cases.explanation: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Respiratory alkalosis. In acute respiratory alkalosis, buffering lowers HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 should fall by 2 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 20.1 mmol/L (acceptable range 18.1-22.1). The measured HCO3 is 11.7 mmol/L. Because the measured HCO3 is below the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present. The anion gap is 137 - (103 + 11.7) = 22.3, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins. No third process is identified. The compensation step suggested a second acid-base process, and the raised anion gap identifies this as HAGMA. Delta gap reasoning does not show an additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis on top of the HAGMA: the bicarbonate level is not clearly lower or higher than expected once the respiratory disorder and raised-gap metabolic acidosis are considered. The overall pattern is respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA. This pattern combines hyperventilation with a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. The respiratory alkalosis can lift the pH toward normal, masking the metabolic acidosis. Sepsis is one context where this mixed pattern can appear. A very low PaCO₂ does not explain everything. Check the anion gap for the hidden metabolic acidosis.

cases.title: Toxic alcohol (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 21-year-old woman is brought in after deliberately drinking an unknown amount of antifreeze in a suicide attempt. She is confused, vomiting, and says her vision has become “blurred and strange.”

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise toxic alcohol ingestion as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The clinical context and only modest lactate of 2.1 mmol/L support toxic alcohol ingestion over isolated lactic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 27.5 mmHg (acceptable range 25.5-29.5). The measured PaCO2 is 28.3 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 139 - (96 + 13) = 30.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Toxic alcohol ingestion can produce a severe raised anion gap metabolic acidosis as acidic metabolites accumulate. The acidosis may be out of proportion to the measured lactate. The key pattern is an unexplained high anion gap acidosis without another clear cause.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A severe unexplained high anion gap acidosis, especially with lactate that does not explain it, should make toxic alcohols ring alarm bells.

cases.title: Severe postictal lactic acidosis
cases.learning_objective: Recognise severe post-ictal lactic acidosis with inadequate respiratory compensation, and use delta-ratio reasoning to identify a defensible concurrent normal anion gap metabolic acidosis.
cases.clinical_stem: A 28-year-old man is witnessed to have a generalised tonic-clonic seizure while in ED. He is quickly brought to a resuscitation bay and a blood gas is taken.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

questions_flow.label: Diagnosis
questions_flow.prompt: What is the most likely interpretation?
options: Mixed metabolic acidosis (HAGMA + NAGMA) + respiratory acidosis
High anion gap metabolic acidosis + respiratory acidosis
High anion gap metabolic acidosis + respiratory alkalosis

answer_key.final_diagnosis: Mixed metabolic acidosis (HAGMA + NAGMA) + respiratory acidosis

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 21.4 mmHg (acceptable range 19.4-23.4). The measured PaCO2 is 39.7 mmHg. Because the measured PaCO2 is above the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion gap
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is approximately 23 mmol/L, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA. In this post-ictal context, the lactate of 19 mmol/L is the likely dominant unmeasured anion.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional metabolic process
explanation_blueprint.body: The delta ratio supports an additional NAGMA. The anion gap has risen by about 7 mmol/L, while the bicarbonate has fallen by about 15 mmol/L, giving a low delta ratio of approximately 0.5. This means the bicarbonate has fallen more than expected for the HAGMA alone, consistent with concurrent hyperchloremic NAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This is a triple acid-base disorder: HAGMA from severe post-ictal lactic acidosis, concurrent NAGMA suggested by the low delta ratio and hyperchloraemia, and respiratory acidosis from inadequate ventilation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical context
explanation_blueprint.body: Generalized seizures can produce dramatic transient lactate elevations due to intense muscular activity and tissue hypoxia. Lactate levels may appear critically abnormal immediately post-ictally but often improve rapidly within 1-2 hours if seizure activity has ceased and perfusion is restored. The teaching trap is that the lactate can dominate attention and make the case look like a simple high anion gap metabolic acidosis. In this gas, the unexpectedly high PaCO2 and the low delta ratio both add important extra information. Persistent or worsening acidosis should prompt consideration of ongoing seizure activity, shock, sepsis, toxic ingestion, or other causes of severe lactic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Severe post-ictal lactic acidosis can produce a dramatic HAGMA, but the full interpretation still comes from the pattern: expected PaCO₂, measured PaCO₂, anion gap, and delta ratio. Here, those checks identify HAGMA + NAGMA + respiratory acidosis.

cases.title: Critical asthma with peri-arrest ventilatory failure
cases.learning_objective: Recognise life-threatening asthma with extreme ventilatory failure, severe respiratory acidosis, oxygenation failure, and concurrent metabolic acidosis after recurrent PEA arrest.
cases.clinical_stem: A middle-aged woman with worsening wheeze became unresponsive prior to ambulance arrival. She had recurrent PEA arrests, requiring intubation and bilateral finger thoracostomies, before ROSC was achieved. She arrives in ED on an adrenaline infusion. This is her ABG.

questions_flow.label: A-a gradient
questions_flow.prompt: Can the observed PaO₂ be explained by hypoventilation alone?
options: Yes - hypoxaemia is mostly explained by reduced alveolar oxygen from hypoventilation
No - there is impaired oxygen transfer from alveoli to arterial blood
There is no meaningful oxygenation abnormality
Cannot assess from the available data

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

questions_flow.label: Diagnosis
questions_flow.prompt: What is the most likely interpretation?
options: Critical asthma with severe ventilatory failure and mixed acidosis
Post-arrest lactic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
Isolated oxygenation failure without ventilatory failure

answer_key.aa_gradient_mechanism: No - there is impaired oxygen transfer from alveoli to arterial blood
answer_key.final_diagnosis: Critical asthma with severe ventilatory failure and mixed acidosis

explanation_blueprint.title: Oxygenation
explanation_blueprint.body: A PaO₂ of 90 mmHg might look acceptable in isolation, but this patient is on FiO₂ 1.0 with an oxygen saturation of 76%. This is severe oxygenation failure, not reassuring oxygenation.

explanation_blueprint.title: A-a gradient
explanation_blueprint.body: Using the alveolar gas equation, PAO₂ ≈ FiO₂ × (760 − 47) − PaCO₂/0.8. Here, PAO₂ is approximately 713 − 285 = 428 mmHg. With a measured PaO₂ of 90 mmHg, the A–a gradient is about 338 mmHg. This is markedly raised, meaning the low PaO₂ cannot be explained by hypoventilation alone; there is impaired oxygen transfer from alveoli to arterial blood.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 18.5 mmHg (acceptable range 16.5-20.5). The measured PaCO2 is 228.0 mmHg. Because the measured PaCO2 is above the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion gap
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 25 mmol/L, which is raised. This indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis, with the lactate of 12.5 mmol/L supporting severe lactic acidosis in the context of cardiac arrest, shock, and critical illness.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional metabolic process
explanation_blueprint.body: The bicarbonate has fallen disproportionately compared with the rise in anion gap. This produces a low delta relationship and supports an additional NAGMA alongside the high anion gap acidosis. The severe CO₂ retention is a separate respiratory process identified in the compensation step.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This is critical asthma with catastrophic ventilatory failure. The extreme PaCO₂ elevation explains the severe respiratory acidosis, while the lactate and low bicarbonate show concurrent metabolic acidosis after recurrent arrest and shock. The overall pattern is mixed acidosis with severe oxygenation and ventilation failure.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical context
explanation_blueprint.body: In severe asthma, a normal or rising PaCO₂ is ominous because it suggests the patient can no longer maintain ventilation. A PaCO₂ of 228 mmHg is extreme ventilatory failure and should be interpreted as a peri-arrest finding.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: In critical asthma, PaCO₂ is the red flag. Severe acidaemia with extreme CO₂ retention means life-threatening ventilatory failure, even when oxygenation and metabolic acidosis are also abnormal.

cases.title: Simple metabolic alkalosis
cases.clinical_stem: A 24F presents with 3 days of persistent vomiting after a viral illness. She is unable to tolerate oral intake and feels lightheaded on standing. This is her blood gas.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise a simple metabolic alkalosis pattern with elevated bicarbonate and compensatory CO2 retention

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 30 mmol/L and has moved up with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic alkalosis. High HCO3 represents excess base in the blood, so it directly pushes the pH upward.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from gastric losses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Metabolic alkalosis occurs when bicarbonate is relatively increased, often from hydrogen ion loss or volume contraction. Chloride is often low because chloride depletion helps maintain the alkalosis. Gastric acid loss from vomiting is a common example.

ph_status.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

primary_disorder.title: Primary Disorder

final_diagnosis.title: Diagnosis
final_diagnosis.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from gastric losses.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 77-year-old woman with chronic type 2 respiratory failure is brought in by ambulance with worsening breathlessness, wheeze, and thicker sputum over the past day. Her family say she seems more tired and drowsy than usual.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 71.0 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis. CO2 behaves like an acid load: as PaCO2 rises, more carbonic acid is generated and the pH falls.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is consistent with an acute on chronic respiratory acidosis. Chronic CO2 retention leads to metabolic compensation, with an acute rise in the PaCO2 during exacerbation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by 4 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 36.4 mmol/L (acceptable range 34.4-38.4). The measured HCO3 is 31.9 mmol/L. The acute-only expected HCO3 is about 27.1 mmol/L (range 25.1-29.1). The measured HCO3 sits between the acute and stable chronic expected responses, supporting acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis without implying a precise combined target.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: An acute COPD exacerbation can worsen ventilation, causing PaCO₂ to rise further and pH to fall. The bicarbonate may already be elevated from chronic compensation, but it has not had time to fully match the acute change. This creates an acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis pattern.

primary_disorder.title: Primary Disorder
primary_disorder.body: PaCO2 is 71.0 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis.

final_diagnosis.title: Diagnosis
final_diagnosis.body: This pattern is consistent with an acute on chronic respiratory acidosis.

compensation.title: Compensation
compensation.body: Expected HCO3 is about 36.4 mmol/L; measured HCO3 is 31.9 mmol/L, so compensation is inappropriate.

Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by 4 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 39.9 mmol/L (acceptable range 37.9-41.9). The measured HCO3 is 36.2 mmol/L. The acute-only expected HCO3 is about 28.0 mmol/L (range 26.0-30.0). The measured HCO3 sits between the acute and stable chronic expected responses, supporting acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis without implying a precise combined target. This pattern is consistent with an acute on chronic respiratory acidosis. Chronic CO2 retention leads to metabolic compensation, with an acute rise in the PaCO2 during exacerbation. An acute COPD exacerbation can worsen ventilation, causing PaCO₂ to rise further and pH to fall. The bicarbonate may already be elevated from chronic compensation, but it has not had time to fully match the acute change. This creates an acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis pattern.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 41-year-old man comes in feeling weak and dizzy after several days of repeated vomiting. He looks dry and has been unable to keep food or fluids down.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 38 mmol/L and has moved up with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic alkalosis. High HCO3 represents excess base in the blood, so it directly pushes the pH upward.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from gastric losses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic alkalosis, the body compensates by hypoventilating to retain PaCO2. Using the metabolic alkalosis compensation rule, the expected PaCO2 is about 49.8 mmHg (acceptable range 46.8-52.8). The measured PaCO2 is 50.5 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Vomiting causes loss of gastric acid, raising bicarbonate and producing metabolic alkalosis. The body compensates by retaining CO₂ through hypoventilation. Ongoing chloride and volume depletion help keep the alkalosis going.

compensation.body: Expected PaCO2 is about 49.8 mmHg; measured PaCO2 is 50.5 mmHg, so compensation is appropriate.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 76-year-old man with long-standing breathlessness and a heavy smoking history comes in more short of breath than usual, with a productive cough and a bit more fatigue. His wife says he is often sleepy in the afternoons and has had poor exercise tolerance for years.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is in the normal range (7.35 - 7.45).

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 55 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis. CO2 behaves like an acid load: as PaCO2 rises, more carbonic acid is generated and the pH falls.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chronic CO2 retention in the setting of COPD.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by 4 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 30.0 mmol/L (acceptable range 28.0-32.0). The measured HCO3 is 30.1 mmol/L. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate metabolic compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Chronic CO₂ retention in COPD leads to a persistently high PaCO₂. Over time, the kidneys retain bicarbonate, bringing the pH closer to normal. This creates a compensated respiratory acidosis pattern.

cases.title: Respiratory acidosis with concurrent HAGMA
cases.clinical_stem: A 57-year-old woman with known COPD is becoming progressively drowsy and more breathless, with shallow breathing and a chesty cough. She looks unwell and is difficult to rouse despite oxygen therapy.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory acidosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 72.2 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis. CO2 behaves like an acid load: as PaCO2 rises, more carbonic acid is generated and the pH falls.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory acidosis with concurrent HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by 4 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 36.9 mmol/L (acceptable range 34.9-38.9). The measured HCO3 is 27.2 mmol/L. Because the measured HCO3 is below the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 143 - (94 + 27.2) = 21.8, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: No third process is identified. The compensation step suggested a second acid-base process, and the raised anion gap identifies this as HAGMA. Delta gap reasoning does not show an additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis on top of the HAGMA: the bicarbonate level is not clearly lower or higher than expected once the respiratory disorder and raised-gap metabolic acidosis are considered.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern combines impaired ventilation with a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. PaCO₂ retention pushes the pH down, while the metabolic acidosis adds a second acidifying process. The result is often a more severe acidaemia than either process alone.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high PaCO₂ plus a raised anion gap means two problems may be happening at once.

cases.title: Severe septic acidosis with profound hypothermia
cases.learning_objective: Recognise severe high anion gap metabolic acidosis in a profoundly hypothermic septic patient, and understand why compensation assessment is more nuanced when interpreting an uncorrected VBG at very low body temperature.
cases.clinical_stem: A 60-year-old woman is brought into ED by ambulance. She is hypothermic with a temperature of 28 degrees Celsius (84 degrees Fahrenheit), tachycardic, and hypotensive. She appears unwell. A venous blood gas was taken when acquiring IV access.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

questions_flow.label: Diagnosis
questions_flow.prompt: What is the most likely interpretation?
options: Sepsis-associated HAGMA with impaired ventilatory compensation
Salicylate toxicity with mixed metabolic acidosis and respiratory alkalosis
DKA with concurrent metabolic alkalosis from vomiting
Cannot be interpreted due to the patient's temperature

answer_key.final_diagnosis: Sepsis-associated HAGMA with impaired ventilatory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: There is profound acidaemia with a pH of 6.88. The bicarbonate is severely reduced at 7 mmol/L, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. The pCO2 is not high enough to explain the degree of acidaemia as an isolated respiratory disorder.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 18.5 mmHg (acceptable range 16.5-20.5). The measured PaCO2 is 33.0 mmHg. Because the measured PaCO2 is above the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present. Because this is a venous sample obtained during profound hypothermia, the exact difference should be interpreted with clinical context, although it remains above the expected range.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion gap
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 36. This is clearly raised, confirming a HAGMA

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional metabolic process
explanation_blueprint.body: The bicarbonate is profoundly low and the anion gap is markedly raised. The delta relationship is broadly consistent with a predominant HAGMA, without a clear additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis. The extra process here is respiratory, not metabolic.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This is severe high anion gap metabolic acidosis in the setting of severe sepsis, with inadequate respiratory compensation. The lactate is elevated at 3.0 mmol/L, but the severity of the acidosis and anion gap should prompt consideration of broader critical illness contributors such as renal impairment, ketones, toxins, or other unmeasured anions depending on the clinical context.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical context
explanation_blueprint.body: A pH of 6.88 is a critical result. In severe metabolic acidosis, the patient should be hyperventilating aggressively to reduce CO2. A pCO2 of 33 mmHg may look deceptively low in isolation, but with a bicarbonate of 7 mmol/L it is dangerously high. The potassium of 5.7 mmol/L is also consistent with severe acidaemia and critical illness.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Profound hypothermia changes how the gas should be interpreted: most analysers report values at 37°C, not the patient’s actual temperature. At 28°C, temperature correction would tend to raise the pH and lower the pCO2, so the exact degree of respiratory compensation is less certain. The key diagnosis remains severe HAGMA.

cases.title: Starvation ketosis (subtle HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 52-year-old woman comes in feeling weak and nauseated after several days of barely eating because of a sore mouth and poor appetite. She has lost weight recently and says she has not been keeping up with fluids.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise starvation ketosis as a subtle high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation and normal-range glucose

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 17 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 3.6 mmol/L, which is not markedly elevated. This is more in keeping with starvation ketosis over DKA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 33.5 mmHg (acceptable range 31.5-35.5). The measured PaCO2 is 34.0 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 140 - (105 + 17) = 18.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Poor oral intake can increase ketone production and cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is usually milder than DKA because some insulin activity remains. The key clue is ketosis with normal or only mildly elevated glucose.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Ketones without major hyperglycaemia are not automatically DKA. The story around the gas matters.

cases.title: Mixed HAGMA and metabolic alkalosis
cases.clinical_stem: A 55-year-old woman has been unwell for several days with persistent vomiting, poor oral intake, and worsening lethargy after a recent febrile illness. She now looks dehydrated and complains of vague upper abdominal discomfort.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise a mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent metabolic alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.36, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 24 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is a mixed HAGMA with concurrent metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 44.0 mmHg (acceptable range 42.0-46.0). The measured PaCO2 is 43.6 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 140 - (91 + 24) = 25.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Delta gap reasoning compares the rise in anion gap with the fall in HCO3. With an anion gap of 25.0, a pure raised-gap metabolic acidosis would usually lower HCO3 to about 11.0 mmol/L. The measured HCO3 is 24 mmol/L, which is higher than expected. This means the bicarbonate has been relatively preserved, indicating an additional metabolic alkalosis. This pattern can occur when acid generation is combined with vomiting or diuretic effect.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern combines acid generation with a process that raises bicarbonate, such as vomiting or diuretic effect. The anion gap reveals the metabolic acidosis, while the higher-than-expected bicarbonate reveals the alkalosis. The key is recognising that both processes can be present at once.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap shows the acidosis; the bicarbonate tells you something is pushing back the other way.

Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 44.0 mmHg (acceptable range 42.0-46.0). The measured PaCO2 is 43.6 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation. The anion gap is 140 - (91 + 24) = 25.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins. Delta gap reasoning compares the rise in anion gap with the fall in HCO3. With an anion gap of 25.0, a pure raised-gap metabolic acidosis would usually lower HCO3 to about 11.0 mmol/L. The measured HCO3 is 24 mmol/L, which is higher than expected. This means the bicarbonate has been relatively preserved, indicating an additional metabolic alkalosis. This pattern can occur when acid generation is combined with vomiting or diuretic effect. The overall pattern is a mixed HAGMA with concurrent metabolic alkalosis. This pattern combines acid generation with a process that raises bicarbonate, such as vomiting or diuretic effect. The anion gap reveals the metabolic acidosis, while the higher-than-expected bicarbonate reveals the alkalosis. The key is recognising that both processes can be present at once. The anion gap shows the acidosis; the bicarbonate tells you something is pushing back the other way.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 34 mmol/L and has moved up with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic alkalosis. High HCO3 represents excess base in the blood, so it directly pushes the pH upward.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from gastric losses.

This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Vomiting causes loss of gastric acid, raising bicarbonate and producing metabolic alkalosis. The body compensates by retaining CO₂ through hypoventilation. Ongoing chloride and volume depletion help keep the alkalosis going.

cases.title: Septic shock with mixed acid-base disorder
cases.learning_objective: Recognise a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with an additional respiratory alkalosis using Winter's formula.
cases.clinical_stem: A 67-year-old man presents with fever, hypotension, tachypnoea, and confusion. A blood gas is obtained shortly after arrival to the emergency department.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

questions_flow.label: Diagnosis
questions_flow.prompt: What is the most likely interpretation?
options: Isolated metabolic acidosis with appropriate compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 26.0 mmHg (acceptable range 24.0-28.0). The measured PaCO2 is 22.0 mmHg. Because the measured PaCO2 is below the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion gap
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 132 - (95 + 12) = 25, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA, with lactate as the likely unmeasured anion in this case.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional metabolic process
explanation_blueprint.body: The delta relationship does not clearly identify an additional metabolic process. The rise in anion gap is broadly matched by the fall in bicarbonate, which fits a predominant HAGMA without convincing evidence of an additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis. The additional process in this case is respiratory, not metabolic.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical context
explanation_blueprint.body: The lactate is 7.8 mmol/L, supporting lactic acidosis. In sepsis, shock, or severe physiological stress, lactate accumulation produces a HAGMA, while increased respiratory drive can produce a concurrent respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, a PaCO2 lower than Winter's expected range is not overcompensation - it is an additional respiratory alkalosis.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 53-year-old man is sent in by his GP after feeling increasingly weak and light-headed since his diuretic dose was increased last week. He also reports a dry mouth and muscle cramps, and looks a bit volume depleted.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 32 mmol/L and has moved up with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic alkalosis. High HCO3 represents excess base in the blood, so it directly pushes the pH upward.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis. Diuretic use causes volume depletion and chloride loss, which maintains the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic alkalosis, the body compensates by hypoventilating to retain PaCO2. Using the metabolic alkalosis compensation rule, the expected PaCO2 is about 45.6 mmHg (acceptable range 42.6-48.6). The measured PaCO2 is 44.5 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diuretics can cause metabolic alkalosis through volume depletion and renal chloride loss. Chloride depletion helps the kidney retain bicarbonate, which maintains the alkalosis. Potassium is often low or low-normal.

cases.title: Sepsis (respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 40-year-old man presents with fever, productive cough, and increasing shortness of breath. He looks unwell, is breathing rapidly, and is becoming confused.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 26.7 mmHg and has moved down while the pH has moved up, so the primary process is respiratory alkalosis. This is the respiratory side of ROME: PaCO2 and pH move in opposite directions, so losing CO2 raises the pH.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits sepsis-related hyperventilation without an added metabolic process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In acute respiratory alkalosis, buffering lowers HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 should fall by 2 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 21.3 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.3-23.3). The measured HCO3 is 21.1 mmol/L. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate metabolic compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Sepsis can increase respiratory drive, causing hyperventilation and a low PaCO₂. This respiratory alkalosis may appear early, before a metabolic acidosis develops. A normal pH can still hide an important acid-base process.

cases.title: Respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA
cases.clinical_stem: A 39-year-old man is noted to be breathing rapidly and looks generally unwell, with vague abdominal discomfort and a mild fever over the past day. He is sweaty, restless, and says he has not been eating or drinking much.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory alkalosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 27.9 mmHg and has moved down while the pH has moved up, so the primary process is respiratory alkalosis. This is the respiratory side of ROME: PaCO2 and pH move in opposite directions, so losing CO2 raises the pH.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In acute respiratory alkalosis, buffering lowers HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 should fall by 2 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 21.6 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.6-23.6). The measured HCO3 is 14.4 mmol/L. Because the measured HCO3 is below the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 139 - (100 + 14.4) = 24.6, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: No third process is identified. The compensation step suggested a second acid-base process, and the raised anion gap identifies this as HAGMA. Delta gap reasoning does not show an additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis on top of the HAGMA: the bicarbonate level is not clearly lower or higher than expected once the respiratory disorder and raised-gap metabolic acidosis are considered.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern combines hyperventilation with a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. The respiratory alkalosis can lift the pH toward normal, masking the metabolic acidosis. Sepsis is one context where this mixed pattern can appear.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A very low PaCO₂ does not explain everything. Check the anion gap for the hidden metabolic acidosis.

cases.title: Opioid toxicity (acute respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 28 year old male is found unresponsive with drug paraphernalia nearby. His GCS is 6 with pinpoint pupils and shallow respirations.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis due to hypoventilation with appropriate compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 55 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis. CO2 behaves like an acid load: as PaCO2 rises, more carbonic acid is generated and the pH falls.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits opioid-related hypoventilation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Opioid toxicity causes respiratory depression, reducing ventilation and allowing CO₂ to build up. The result is respiratory acidosis with a high PaCO₂ and low pH. The gas pattern reflects inadequate ventilation rather than a primary metabolic problem.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 59-year-old man with long-standing breathlessness and a heavy smoking history comes in more short of breath than usual, with a worsening productive cough. He is using his inhalers more often and seems a little drowsy than baseline.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is in the normal range (7.35 - 7.45).

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 59 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis. CO2 behaves like an acid load: as PaCO2 rises, more carbonic acid is generated and the pH falls.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chronic CO2 retention in the setting of COPD.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by 4 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 31.6 mmol/L (acceptable range 29.6-33.6). The measured HCO3 is 32.0 mmol/L. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate metabolic compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Chronic CO₂ retention in COPD leads to a persistently high PaCO₂. Over time, the kidneys retain bicarbonate, bringing the pH closer to normal. This creates a compensated respiratory acidosis pattern.

primary_disorder.title: Primary Disorder

final_diagnosis.title: Diagnosis

compensation.body: Expected HCO3 is about 31.6 mmol/L; measured HCO3 is 32.0 mmol/L, so compensation is appropriate.
