cases.title: Starvation ketosis (subtle HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 24-year-old man presents with several days of very poor oral intake after a brief vomiting illness. He feels weak, nauseated, and mildly dehydrated, and says he has barely eaten since then.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise starvation ketosis as a subtle high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation and normal-range glucose

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 18 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 3.9 mmol/L, which is not markedly elevated. This is more in keeping with starvation ketosis over DKA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 35.0 mmHg (acceptable range 33.0-37.0). The measured PaCO2 is 35.0 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 139 - (103 + 18) = 18.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Poor oral intake can increase ketone production and cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is usually milder than DKA because some insulin activity remains. The key clue is ketosis with normal or only mildly elevated glucose.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Ketones without major hyperglycaemia are not automatically DKA. The story around the gas matters.

cases.clinical_stem: A 29-year-old man is brought in after several episodes of vomiting and says he has missed his insulin for the past day. He is thirsty, looks dehydrated, and is breathing faster than usual with mild abdominal discomfort.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This fits DKA with a concurrent metabolic alkalosis from vomiting rather than an isolated ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 27.5 mmHg (acceptable range 25.5-29.5). The measured PaCO2 is 28.5 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 137 - (95 + 13) = 29.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Delta gap reasoning compares the rise in anion gap with the fall in HCO3. With an anion gap of 29.0, a pure raised-gap metabolic acidosis would usually lower HCO3 to about 7.0 mmol/L. The measured HCO3 is 13 mmol/L, which is higher than expected. This means the bicarbonate has been relatively preserved, indicating an additional metabolic alkalosis. Vomiting is the likely source of the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: DKA causes a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis from ketone production. Vomiting can add a metabolic alkalosis, making the bicarbonate higher than expected. This can make the acidosis look less severe than it really is.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: In DKA, a not-too-low bicarbonate is not always reassuring. Vomiting can hide how bad the acidosis really is.

cases.title: Respiratory acidosis with concurrent HAGMA
cases.clinical_stem: A 47-year-old man is brought in by family because he has become increasingly drowsy and short of breath over the past day. They also report poor intake, fever, and that he has looked generally unwell since yesterday.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory acidosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 58.0 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis. CO2 behaves like an acid load: as PaCO2 rises, more carbonic acid is generated and the pH falls.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory acidosis with concurrent HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by 4 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 31.2 mmol/L (acceptable range 29.2-33.2). The measured HCO3 is 25.9 mmol/L. Because the measured HCO3 is below the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 136 - (92 + 25.9) = 18.1, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: No third process is identified. The compensation step suggested a second acid-base process, and the raised anion gap identifies this as HAGMA. Delta gap reasoning does not show an additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis on top of the HAGMA: the bicarbonate level is not clearly lower or higher than expected once the respiratory disorder and raised-gap metabolic acidosis are considered.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern combines impaired ventilation with a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. PaCO₂ retention pushes the pH down, while the metabolic acidosis adds a second acidifying process. The result is often a more severe acidaemia than either process alone.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high PaCO₂ plus a raised anion gap means two problems may be happening at once.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 27-year-old man comes in feeling light-headed and weak after several days of frequent loose stools and poor oral intake. He also reports crampy lower abdominal discomfort and has a dry mouth.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 19 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss leads to a metabolic acidosis, reflected by a low bicarbonate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 36.5 mmHg (acceptable range 34.5-38.5). The measured PaCO2 is 37.4 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 138 - (110 + 19) = 9.0, which is normal. This makes a raised-gap metabolic acidosis less likely.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diarrhoea causes normal anion gap metabolic acidosis through direct bicarbonate loss from the gut. Chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality, creating a hyperchloraemic acidosis. The pattern may occur with volume depletion and low potassium.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A normal anion gap acidosis points away from hidden acids and toward bicarbonate loss — often from the gut.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 66-year-old woman arrives pale and confused with fever, abdominal pain, and cool peripheries. She is tachycardic and looks poorly perfused despite fluids.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 10.7 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactate is 5.2 mmol/L, which supports lactic acidosis in the septic clinical context.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 24.0 mmHg (acceptable range 22.0-26.0). The measured PaCO2 is 22.1 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 141 - (97 + 10.7) = 33.3, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactic acidosis occurs when lactate accumulates from impaired tissue oxygen delivery or cellular stress. This produces a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Common contexts include sepsis, shock, severe anaemia, or ischaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactate may explain the gap, but do not stop there — check whether the rest of the gas still makes sense.

cases.title: Respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA
cases.clinical_stem: A 49-year-old woman is brought in feeling unwell with rapid breathing, nausea, and a vague abdominal discomfort. She has also had poor oral intake and has looked increasingly weak over the past day.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory alkalosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.42, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 28.6 mmHg and has moved down while the pH has moved up, so the primary process is respiratory alkalosis. This is the respiratory side of ROME: PaCO2 and pH move in opposite directions, so losing CO2 raises the pH.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In acute respiratory alkalosis, buffering lowers HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 should fall by 2 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 21.7 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.7-23.7). The measured HCO3 is 17.9 mmol/L. Because the measured HCO3 is below the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 137 - (101 + 17.9) = 18.1, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: No third process is identified. The compensation step suggested a second acid-base process, and the raised anion gap identifies this as HAGMA. Delta gap reasoning does not show an additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis on top of the HAGMA: the bicarbonate level is not clearly lower or higher than expected once the respiratory disorder and raised-gap metabolic acidosis are considered.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern combines hyperventilation with a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. The respiratory alkalosis can lift the pH toward normal, masking the metabolic acidosis. Sepsis is one context where this mixed pattern can appear.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A very low PaCO₂ does not explain everything. Check the anion gap for the hidden metabolic acidosis.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 67-year-old woman with a long smoking history is noted to be more breathless than usual, with a chronic productive cough and wheeze. Her family say she has had poor exercise tolerance for years and seems a little more drowsy today.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is in the normal range (7.35 - 7.45).

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 62 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis. CO2 behaves like an acid load: as PaCO2 rises, more carbonic acid is generated and the pH falls.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chronic CO2 retention in the setting of COPD.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by 4 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 32.8 mmol/L (acceptable range 30.8-34.8). The measured HCO3 is 33.0 mmol/L. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate metabolic compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Chronic CO₂ retention in COPD leads to a persistently high PaCO₂. Over time, the kidneys retain bicarbonate, bringing the pH closer to normal. This creates a compensated respiratory acidosis pattern.

cases.title: Simple normal anion gap metabolic acidosis
cases.clinical_stem: 31F presents to ED with several days of diarrhoea and reduced oral intake. Her child is currently unwell with similar symptoms. She is light-headed and tired. Looks dry on arrival but has not had vomiting or abdominal pain.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise a simple bicarbonate-driven metabolic acidosis with a normal anion gap

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 16 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss leads to a metabolic acidosis, reflected by a low bicarbonate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Normal anion gap metabolic acidosis usually comes from bicarbonate loss or impaired renal acid excretion. As bicarbonate falls, chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality, creating a hyperchloraemic pattern. Gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss, such as diarrhoea, is a common cause.

cases.title: Alcoholic ketoacidosis (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 30-year-old man with heavy alcohol use has had two days of vomiting and poor oral intake after a flu-like illness, and now looks dehydrated and shaky.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise alcoholic ketoacidosis as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 14 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The history pattern and the HAGMA fit alcoholic ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 29.0 mmHg (acceptable range 27.0-31.0). The measured PaCO2 is 30.6 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 135 - (93 + 14) = 28.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Alcoholic ketoacidosis occurs when heavy alcohol use and poor intake drive ketone production. Glucose is often normal or low, which helps separate it from DKA. Vomiting may add a metabolic alkalosis and partially mask the acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis without a very high glucose should make you think beyond DKA — alcoholic ketoacidosis is one of the big ones.

cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 77-year-old man has become progressively tired, nauseated, and confused over the past day, with very little urine passed. His family says he has not been himself and is now drowsy and weak.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is most consistent with uraemic HAGMA from renal failure.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 27.5 mmHg (acceptable range 25.5-29.5). The measured PaCO2 is 28.4 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 136 - (99 + 13) = 24.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Uraemia causes a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis because impaired renal excretion allows unmeasured acids to accumulate. Bicarbonate falls as these acids are buffered. Respiratory compensation lowers PaCO₂.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Renal failure can explain a raised anion gap, but the compensation still has to fit before you call it a single process.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 67-year-old man comes in feeling weak and light-headed after several days of frequent loose stools and poor oral intake. He also reports abdominal cramping and looks mildly dehydrated.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 18 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss leads to a metabolic acidosis, reflected by a low bicarbonate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 35.0 mmHg (acceptable range 33.0-37.0). The measured PaCO2 is 34.2 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 140 - (110 + 18) = 12.0, which is normal. This makes a raised-gap metabolic acidosis less likely.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diarrhoea causes normal anion gap metabolic acidosis through direct bicarbonate loss from the gut. Chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality, creating a hyperchloraemic acidosis. The pattern may occur with volume depletion and low potassium.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A normal anion gap acidosis points away from hidden acids and toward bicarbonate loss — often from the gut.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 35-year-old man presents with persistent vomiting, weakness, and dizziness after several days of poor oral intake. He has dry mucous membranes and feels light-headed when standing.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 34 mmol/L and has moved up with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic alkalosis. High HCO3 represents excess base in the blood, so it directly pushes the pH upward.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from gastric losses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic alkalosis, the body compensates by hypoventilating to retain PaCO2. Using the metabolic alkalosis compensation rule, the expected PaCO2 is about 47.0 mmHg (acceptable range 44.0-50.0). The measured PaCO2 is 46.0 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 139 - (95 + 34) = 10.0, which is normal. This makes a raised-gap metabolic acidosis less likely.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Vomiting causes loss of gastric acid, raising bicarbonate and producing metabolic alkalosis. The body compensates by retaining CO₂ through hypoventilation. Ongoing chloride and volume depletion help keep the alkalosis going.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Vomiting removes stomach acid, but chloride depletion is what helps the alkalosis stick around.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 63-year-old man with long-standing smoking-related breathlessness comes in more short of breath than usual after a few days of increased cough and sputum. He is wheezy, uses inhalers regularly, and seems a little sleepy but arousable.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 63 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis. CO2 behaves like an acid load: as PaCO2 rises, more carbonic acid is generated and the pH falls.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chronic CO2 retention in the setting of COPD.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by 4 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 33.2 mmol/L (acceptable range 31.2-35.2). The measured HCO3 is 33.0 mmol/L. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate metabolic compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Chronic CO₂ retention in COPD leads to a persistently high PaCO₂. Over time, the kidneys retain bicarbonate, bringing the pH closer to normal. This creates a compensated respiratory acidosis pattern.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: A 5-year-old girl is brought in by her distressed parents after they accidentally applied oil of wintergreen to her skin. She has become sweaty, nauseated, and is breathing very fast, with the parents mentioning she seems uncomfortable and keeps complaining of a funny noise in her ears.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the classic respiratory alkalosis plus high anion gap metabolic acidosis pattern of salicylate toxicity

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 22.4 mmHg and has moved down while the pH has moved up, so the primary process is respiratory alkalosis. This is the respiratory side of ROME: PaCO2 and pH move in opposite directions, so losing CO2 raises the pH.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This is the classic salicylate pattern: respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In acute respiratory alkalosis, buffering lowers HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 should fall by 2 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 20.5 mmol/L (acceptable range 18.5-22.5). The measured HCO3 is 16.7 mmol/L. Because the measured HCO3 is below the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 139 - (103 + 16.7) = 19.3, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: No third process is identified. The compensation step suggested a second acid-base process, and the raised anion gap identifies this as HAGMA. Delta gap reasoning does not show an additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis on top of the HAGMA: the bicarbonate level is not clearly lower or higher than expected once the respiratory disorder and raised-gap metabolic acidosis are considered.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Salicylate toxicity classically causes both respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis. The two processes can partially offset each other, so the pH may look deceptively normal. The mixed pattern is the important clue.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Salicylates are sneaky: alkalosis and acidosis can happen at the same time, sometimes with a deceptively normal pH.

cases.title: Panic / hyperventilation (acute respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 30 year old female presents to ED after almost being struck by a car. She is anxious, has chest tightness and rapid breathing. Oxygen saturations are normal.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits acute hyperventilation such as panic.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Hyperventilation increases CO₂ clearance, causing PaCO₂ to fall and pH to rise. Because this is usually acute, bicarbonate has little time to change. The pattern is respiratory alkalosis driven by increased breathing rather than a primary metabolic process.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 32-year-old woman has become increasingly confused and sweaty overnight, with abdominal pain and cold, mottled hands. She is tachycardic and looks markedly unwell despite being given fluids.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactate is 4.9 mmol/L, which supports lactic acidosis in the septic clinical context.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 30.5 mmHg (acceptable range 28.5-32.5). The measured PaCO2 is 30.3 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 141 - (96 + 15.0) = 30.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactic acidosis occurs when lactate accumulates from impaired tissue oxygen delivery or cellular stress. This produces a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Common contexts include sepsis, shock, severe anaemia, or ischaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactate may explain the gap, but do not stop there — check whether the rest of the gas still makes sense.

cases.title: Respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA
cases.clinical_stem: A 69-year-old man looks unwell on the ward with rapid breathing, low-grade fever, and a few hours of increasing confusion. He also mentions vague abdominal discomfort and has been off his food since yesterday.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory alkalosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.41, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 21.3 mmHg and has moved down while the pH has moved up, so the primary process is respiratory alkalosis. This is the respiratory side of ROME: PaCO2 and pH move in opposite directions, so losing CO2 raises the pH.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In acute respiratory alkalosis, buffering lowers HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 should fall by 2 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 20.3 mmol/L (acceptable range 18.3-22.3). The measured HCO3 is 12.9 mmol/L. Because the measured HCO3 is below the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 137 - (100 + 12.9) = 24.1, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: No third process is identified. The compensation step suggested a second acid-base process, and the raised anion gap identifies this as HAGMA. Delta gap reasoning does not show an additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis on top of the HAGMA: the bicarbonate level is not clearly lower or higher than expected once the respiratory disorder and raised-gap metabolic acidosis are considered.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern combines hyperventilation with a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. The respiratory alkalosis can lift the pH toward normal, masking the metabolic acidosis. Sepsis is one context where this mixed pattern can appear.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A very low PaCO₂ does not explain everything. Check the anion gap for the hidden metabolic acidosis.

cases.title: Sepsis (respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 34-year-old man arrives looking unwell with fever, a productive cough, and marked shortness of breath. He is pale, confused, and breathing fast, with a low blood pressure on arrival.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 27.2 mmHg and has moved down while the pH has moved up, so the primary process is respiratory alkalosis. This is the respiratory side of ROME: PaCO2 and pH move in opposite directions, so losing CO2 raises the pH.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits sepsis-related hyperventilation without an added metabolic process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In acute respiratory alkalosis, buffering lowers HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 should fall by 2 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 21.4 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.4-23.4). The measured HCO3 is 21.0 mmol/L. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate metabolic compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Sepsis can increase respiratory drive, causing hyperventilation and a low PaCO₂. This respiratory alkalosis may appear early, before a metabolic acidosis develops. A normal pH can still hide an important acid-base process.

cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)
cases.clinical_stem: A 20-year-old man comes in after several hours of repeated vomiting and crampy abdominal pain, with his mother saying he has seemed very thirsty and lost weight over recent weeks. He is breathing fast and looks dehydrated.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 18 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This fits DKA with a concurrent metabolic alkalosis from vomiting rather than an isolated ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 35.0 mmHg (acceptable range 33.0-37.0). The measured PaCO2 is 34.7 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 137 - (91 + 18) = 28.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Delta gap reasoning compares the rise in anion gap with the fall in HCO3. With an anion gap of 28.0, a pure raised-gap metabolic acidosis would usually lower HCO3 to about 8.0 mmol/L. The measured HCO3 is 18 mmol/L, which is higher than expected. This means the bicarbonate has been relatively preserved, indicating an additional metabolic alkalosis. Vomiting is the likely source of the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: DKA causes a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis from ketone production. Vomiting can add a metabolic alkalosis, making the bicarbonate higher than expected. This can make the acidosis look less severe than it really is.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: In DKA, a not-too-low bicarbonate is not always reassuring. Vomiting can hide how bad the acidosis really is.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 62-year-old man with long-term smoking-related breathlessness is a little more sleepy than usual and says his cough and wheeze have been worse over the past few days. He struggles to walk across a room without stopping and has brought up more sputum than usual.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 65 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis. CO2 behaves like an acid load: as PaCO2 rises, more carbonic acid is generated and the pH falls.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chronic CO2 retention in the setting of COPD.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by 4 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 34.0 mmol/L (acceptable range 32.0-36.0). The measured HCO3 is 34.0 mmol/L. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate metabolic compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Chronic CO₂ retention in COPD leads to a persistently high PaCO₂. Over time, the kidneys retain bicarbonate, bringing the pH closer to normal. This creates a compensated respiratory acidosis pattern.

compensation.body: Expected HCO3 is about 34.0 mmol/L; measured HCO3 is 34.0 mmol/L, so compensation is appropriate.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 31-year-old man is brought in feeling weak and increasingly thirsty after several days of feverish cough and poor oral intake. He has been vomiting intermittently and says his abdominal pain has got worse today.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 10 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 24.9 mmol/L, which strongly supports DKA as the cause of this HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 23.0 mmHg (acceptable range 21.0-25.0). The measured PaCO2 is 23.2 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 136 - (102 + 10) = 24.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: DKA is driven by insulin deficiency, which leads to ketone production and a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Marked hyperglycaemia helps distinguish it from other ketoacidoses. Dehydration and electrolyte shifts often accompany the acid-base disturbance.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: DKA usually gives you a high anion gap acidosis with high glucose — but still check whether the CO₂ and bicarbonate behave the way they should.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis

cases.title: Simple normal anion gap metabolic acidosis

cases.learning_objective: Recognise a simple bicarbonate-driven metabolic acidosis with a normal anion gap

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 16 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss leads to a metabolic acidosis, reflected by a low bicarbonate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Normal anion gap metabolic acidosis usually comes from bicarbonate loss or impaired renal acid excretion. As bicarbonate falls, chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality, creating a hyperchloraemic pattern. Gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss, such as diarrhoea, is a common cause.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 67-year-old woman presents with several days of nausea, retching, and progressive weakness. She has been unable to keep down food or fluids and is dizzy when upright.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 36 mmol/L and has moved up with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic alkalosis. High HCO3 represents excess base in the blood, so it directly pushes the pH upward.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from gastric losses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic alkalosis, the body compensates by hypoventilating to retain PaCO2. Using the metabolic alkalosis compensation rule, the expected PaCO2 is about 48.4 mmHg (acceptable range 45.4-51.4). The measured PaCO2 is 47.5 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 141 - (96 + 36) = 9.0, which is normal. This makes a raised-gap metabolic acidosis less likely.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Vomiting causes loss of gastric acid, raising bicarbonate and producing metabolic alkalosis. The body compensates by retaining CO₂ through hypoventilation. Ongoing chloride and volume depletion help keep the alkalosis going.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Vomiting removes stomach acid, but chloride depletion is what helps the alkalosis stick around.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis

cases.title: Respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA
cases.clinical_stem: A 33-year-old woman presents with rapid breathing, feeling generally unwell, and poor oral intake over the past day. She also reports nausea and mild abdominal discomfort.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory alkalosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.39, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 31.1 mmHg and has moved down while the pH has moved up, so the primary process is respiratory alkalosis. This is the respiratory side of ROME: PaCO2 and pH move in opposite directions, so losing CO2 raises the pH.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In acute respiratory alkalosis, buffering lowers HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 should fall by 2 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 22.2 mmol/L (acceptable range 20.2-24.2). The measured HCO3 is 18.2 mmol/L. Because the measured HCO3 is below the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 141 - (102 + 18.2) = 20.8, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: No third process is identified. The compensation step suggested a second acid-base process, and the raised anion gap identifies this as HAGMA. Delta gap reasoning does not show an additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis on top of the HAGMA: the bicarbonate level is not clearly lower or higher than expected once the respiratory disorder and raised-gap metabolic acidosis are considered.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern combines hyperventilation with a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. The respiratory alkalosis can lift the pH toward normal, masking the metabolic acidosis. Sepsis is one context where this mixed pattern can appear.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A very low PaCO₂ does not explain everything. Check the anion gap for the hidden metabolic acidosis.

cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 63-year-old woman has become increasingly tired and nauseated over the past few days, with noticeably reduced urine output. Her family say she seems more confused and weak than usual.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 12 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is most consistent with uraemic HAGMA from renal failure.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 26.0 mmHg (acceptable range 24.0-28.0). The measured PaCO2 is 25.5 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 138 - (98 + 12) = 28.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Uraemia causes a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis because impaired renal excretion allows unmeasured acids to accumulate. Bicarbonate falls as these acids are buffered. Respiratory compensation lowers PaCO₂.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Renal failure can explain a raised anion gap, but the compensation still has to fit before you call it a single process.

cases.title: Respiratory acidosis with concurrent HAGMA
cases.clinical_stem: A 44-year-old man is brought in with increasing breathlessness and drowsiness after a few days of vomiting and poor intake. He looks unwell, is tachypnoeic, and is becoming harder to rouse.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory acidosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 62.5 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis. CO2 behaves like an acid load: as PaCO2 rises, more carbonic acid is generated and the pH falls.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory acidosis with concurrent HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by 4 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 33.0 mmol/L (acceptable range 31.0-35.0). The measured HCO3 is 27.3 mmol/L. Because the measured HCO3 is below the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 136 - (92 + 27.3) = 16.7, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: No third process is identified. The compensation step suggested a second acid-base process, and the raised anion gap identifies this as HAGMA. Delta gap reasoning does not show an additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis on top of the HAGMA: the bicarbonate level is not clearly lower or higher than expected once the respiratory disorder and raised-gap metabolic acidosis are considered.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern combines impaired ventilation with a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. PaCO₂ retention pushes the pH down, while the metabolic acidosis adds a second acidifying process. The result is often a more severe acidaemia than either process alone.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high PaCO₂ plus a raised anion gap means two problems may be happening at once.

explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 36.5 mmHg (acceptable range 34.5-38.5). The measured PaCO2 is 37.7 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 138 - (109 + 19) = 10.0, which is normal. This makes a raised-gap metabolic acidosis less likely.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diarrhoea causes normal anion gap metabolic acidosis through direct bicarbonate loss from the gut. Chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality, creating a hyperchloraemic acidosis. The pattern may occur with volume depletion and low potassium.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A normal anion gap acidosis points away from hidden acids and toward bicarbonate loss — often from the gut.

cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: An 81-year-old woman has become increasingly tired and nauseated over several days, with reduced urine output. Her family also reports mild confusion and poor appetite.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 12 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is most consistent with uraemic HAGMA from renal failure.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 26.0 mmHg (acceptable range 24.0-28.0). The measured PaCO2 is 26.1 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 136 - (101 + 12) = 23.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Uraemia causes a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis because impaired renal excretion allows unmeasured acids to accumulate. Bicarbonate falls as these acids are buffered. Respiratory compensation lowers PaCO₂.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Renal failure can explain a raised anion gap, but the compensation still has to fit before you call it a single process.

cases.title: Mixed HAGMA and metabolic alkalosis
cases.clinical_stem: A 33-year-old man presents with several days of persistent vomiting, poor oral intake, and worsening upper abdominal pain after a recent febrile illness. He looks dehydrated and says he has felt progressively weaker and more lightheaded on standing.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise a mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent metabolic alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.35, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 23 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is a mixed HAGMA with concurrent metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 42.5 mmHg (acceptable range 40.5-44.5). The measured PaCO2 is 42.8 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 142 - (96 + 23) = 23.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Delta gap reasoning compares the rise in anion gap with the fall in HCO3. With an anion gap of 23.0, a pure raised-gap metabolic acidosis would usually lower HCO3 to about 13.0 mmol/L. The measured HCO3 is 23 mmol/L, which is higher than expected. This means the bicarbonate has been relatively preserved, indicating an additional metabolic alkalosis. This pattern can occur when acid generation is combined with vomiting or diuretic effect.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern combines acid generation with a process that raises bicarbonate, such as vomiting or diuretic effect. The anion gap reveals the metabolic acidosis, while the higher-than-expected bicarbonate reveals the alkalosis. The key is recognising that both processes can be present at once.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap shows the acidosis; the bicarbonate tells you something is pushing back the other way.

cases.title: Sepsis (respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 55-year-old woman attends ED with fever, rigors, right upper quadrant abdominal pain, and increasing confusion. She is warm peripherally, tachycardic, and markedly tachypnoeic.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 24.9 mmHg and has moved down while the pH has moved up, so the primary process is respiratory alkalosis. This is the respiratory side of ROME: PaCO2 and pH move in opposite directions, so losing CO2 raises the pH.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits sepsis-related hyperventilation without an added metabolic process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In acute respiratory alkalosis, buffering lowers HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 should fall by 2 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 21.0 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.0-23.0). The measured HCO3 is 20.9 mmol/L. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate metabolic compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 138 - (105 + 20.9) = 12.1, which is normal. This makes a raised-gap metabolic acidosis less likely.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Sepsis can increase respiratory drive, causing hyperventilation and a low PaCO₂. This respiratory alkalosis may appear early, before a metabolic acidosis develops. A normal pH can still hide an important acid-base process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A low PaCO₂ in a sick patient can be an early warning sign. A normal pH does not mean a normal gas.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 68-year-old woman with severe COPD presents with worsening breathlessness, purulent sputum, and increasing drowsiness. She is using accessory muscles and can only speak in short phrases.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 84.2 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis. CO2 behaves like an acid load: as PaCO2 rises, more carbonic acid is generated and the pH falls.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is consistent with an acute on chronic respiratory acidosis. Chronic CO2 retention leads to metabolic compensation, with an acute rise in the PaCO2 during exacerbation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by 4 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 41.7 mmol/L (acceptable range 39.7-43.7). The measured HCO3 is 38.5 mmol/L. The acute-only expected HCO3 is about 28.4 mmol/L (range 26.4-30.4). The measured HCO3 sits between the acute and stable chronic expected responses, supporting acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis without implying a precise combined target.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 136 - (99 + 38.5) = -1.5, which is normal. This makes a raised-gap metabolic acidosis less likely.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: An acute COPD exacerbation can worsen ventilation, causing PaCO₂ to rise further and pH to fall. The bicarbonate may already be elevated from chronic compensation, but it has not had time to fully match the acute change. This creates an acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis pattern.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: If the PaCO₂ has risen but the bicarbonate has not caught up, think acute-on-chronic respiratory failure.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 71-year-old woman with long-standing smoking-related lung problems is reviewed for worsening shortness of breath and a cough with more sputum than usual. Her family says she has been more sleepy than normal and struggles to walk across the room without stopping.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is in the normal range (7.35 - 7.45).

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 62 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis. CO2 behaves like an acid load: as PaCO2 rises, more carbonic acid is generated and the pH falls.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chronic CO2 retention in the setting of COPD.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by 4 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 32.8 mmol/L (acceptable range 30.8-34.8). The measured HCO3 is 33.0 mmol/L. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate metabolic compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Chronic CO₂ retention in COPD leads to a persistently high PaCO₂. Over time, the kidneys retain bicarbonate, bringing the pH closer to normal. This creates a compensated respiratory acidosis pattern.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: An 8-year-old girl is brought in by her distressed mother after the child was accidentally given oil of wintergreen for a sore throat at home. She now has vomiting, sweating, and rapid breathing, and her ears have started ringing.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the classic respiratory alkalosis plus high anion gap metabolic acidosis pattern of salicylate toxicity

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.39, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 26.0 mmHg and has moved down while the pH has moved up, so the primary process is respiratory alkalosis. This is the respiratory side of ROME: PaCO2 and pH move in opposite directions, so losing CO2 raises the pH.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This is the classic salicylate pattern: respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In acute respiratory alkalosis, buffering lowers HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 should fall by 2 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 21.2 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.2-23.2). The measured HCO3 is 15.2 mmol/L. Because the measured HCO3 is below the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 140 - (98 + 15.2) = 26.8, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: No third process is identified. The compensation step suggested a second acid-base process, and the raised anion gap identifies this as HAGMA. Delta gap reasoning does not show an additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis on top of the HAGMA: the bicarbonate level is not clearly lower or higher than expected once the respiratory disorder and raised-gap metabolic acidosis are considered.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Salicylate toxicity classically causes both respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis. The two processes can partially offset each other, so the pH may look deceptively normal. The mixed pattern is the important clue.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Salicylates are sneaky: alkalosis and acidosis can happen at the same time, sometimes with a deceptively normal pH.

The measured HCO3 is 15.2 mmol/L. Because the measured HCO3 is below the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present. The anion gap is 140 - (98 + 15.2) = 26.8, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins. No third process is identified. The compensation step suggested a second acid-base process, and the raised anion gap identifies this as HAGMA. Delta gap reasoning does not show an additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis on top of the HAGMA: the bicarbonate level is not clearly lower or higher than expected once the respiratory disorder and raised-gap metabolic acidosis are considered. This is the classic salicylate pattern: respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA. Salicylate toxicity classically causes both respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis. The two processes can partially offset each other, so the pH may look deceptively normal. The mixed pattern is the important clue. Salicylates are sneaky: alkalosis and acidosis can happen at the same time, sometimes with a deceptively normal pH.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 65M with known COPD attends ED with worsening wheeze, productive cough, morning headaches, and confusion. His partner says he has become increasingly sleepy today.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 81.3 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis. CO2 behaves like an acid load: as PaCO2 rises, more carbonic acid is generated and the pH falls.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is consistent with an acute on chronic respiratory acidosis. Chronic CO2 retention leads to metabolic compensation, with an acute rise in the PaCO2 during exacerbation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by 4 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 40.5 mmol/L (acceptable range 38.5-42.5). The measured HCO3 is 36.7 mmol/L. The acute-only expected HCO3 is about 28.1 mmol/L (range 26.1-30.1). The measured HCO3 sits between the acute and stable chronic expected responses, supporting acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis without implying a precise combined target.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 140 - (100 + 36.7) = 3.3, which is normal. This makes a raised-gap metabolic acidosis less likely.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: An acute COPD exacerbation can worsen ventilation, causing PaCO₂ to rise further and pH to fall. The bicarbonate may already be elevated from chronic compensation, but it has not had time to fully match the acute change. This creates an acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis pattern.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: If the PaCO₂ has risen but the bicarbonate has not caught up, think acute-on-chronic respiratory failure.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 67-year-old woman has been vomiting repeatedly for several days and now feels weak and dizzy. She has barely been able to keep fluids down and looks dehydrated.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 32 mmol/L and has moved up with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic alkalosis. High HCO3 represents excess base in the blood, so it directly pushes the pH upward.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from gastric losses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic alkalosis, the body compensates by hypoventilating to retain PaCO2. Using the metabolic alkalosis compensation rule, the expected PaCO2 is about 45.6 mmHg (acceptable range 42.6-48.6). The measured PaCO2 is 44.2 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Vomiting causes loss of gastric acid, raising bicarbonate and producing metabolic alkalosis. The body compensates by retaining CO₂ through hypoventilation. Ongoing chloride and volume depletion help keep the alkalosis going.

primary_disorder.title: Primary Disorder

final_diagnosis.title: Diagnosis
