compensation.title: Compensation
compensation.body: Expected PaCO2 is about 45.6 mmHg; measured PaCO2 is 44.2 mmHg, so compensation is appropriate.

cases.title: Simple normal anion gap metabolic acidosis
cases.clinical_stem: A 24 year old female who recently returned from Bali presents with several days of profuse diarrhoea. She is clinically dry with a mild tachycardia, but is otherwise well.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise a simple bicarbonate-driven metabolic acidosis with a normal anion gap

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 17 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss leads to a metabolic acidosis, reflected by a low bicarbonate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Normal anion gap metabolic acidosis usually comes from bicarbonate loss or impaired renal acid excretion. As bicarbonate falls, chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality, creating a hyperchloraemic pattern. Gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss, such as diarrhoea, is a common cause.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: An acute COPD exacerbation can worsen ventilation, causing PaCO₂ to rise further and pH to fall. cases.title: Respiratory acidosis with concurrent HAGMA
cases.clinical_stem: A 42-year-old man is brought in drowsy and increasingly short of breath, with shallow breathing and a strong smell of ketones on his breath. He looks acutely unwell and is too lethargic to give a clear history.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory acidosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 69.1 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis. CO2 behaves like an acid load: as PaCO2 rises, more carbonic acid is generated and the pH falls.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory acidosis with concurrent HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by 4 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 35.6 mmol/L (acceptable range 33.6-37.6). The measured HCO3 is 22.7 mmol/L. Because the measured HCO3 is below the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 140 - (93 + 22.7) = 24.3, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: No third process is identified. The compensation step suggested a second acid-base process, and the raised anion gap identifies this as HAGMA. Delta gap reasoning does not show an additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis on top of the HAGMA: the bicarbonate level is not clearly lower or higher than expected once the respiratory disorder and raised-gap metabolic acidosis are considered.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern combines impaired ventilation with a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. PaCO₂ retention pushes the pH down, while the metabolic acidosis adds a second acidifying process. The result is often a more severe acidaemia than either process alone.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high PaCO₂ plus a raised anion gap means two problems may be happening at once.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: A 14-year-old boy is brought in after his parents accidentally rubbed oil of wintergreen on his skin, and his mother is worried he may have also licked some from the bottle. He is sweaty, vomiting, and says his ears are ringing.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the classic respiratory alkalosis plus high anion gap metabolic acidosis pattern of salicylate toxicity

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.39, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 26.4 mmHg and has moved down while the pH has moved up, so the primary process is respiratory alkalosis. This is the respiratory side of ROME: PaCO2 and pH move in opposite directions, so losing CO2 raises the pH.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This is the classic salicylate pattern: respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In acute respiratory alkalosis, buffering lowers HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 should fall by 2 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 21.3 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.3-23.3). The measured HCO3 is 15.3 mmol/L. Because the measured HCO3 is below the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 142 - (106 + 15.3) = 20.7, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: No third process is identified. The compensation step suggested a second acid-base process, and the raised anion gap identifies this as HAGMA. Delta gap reasoning does not show an additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis on top of the HAGMA: the bicarbonate level is not clearly lower or higher than expected once the respiratory disorder and raised-gap metabolic acidosis are considered.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Salicylate toxicity classically causes both respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis. The two processes can partially offset each other, so the pH may look deceptively normal. The mixed pattern is the important clue.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Salicylates are sneaky: alkalosis and acidosis can happen at the same time, sometimes with a deceptively normal pH.

cases.title: DKA with inadequate respiratory compensation

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 19-year-old woman comes in feeling generally unwell with abdominal pain, thirst, and deep rapid breathing. She says she has missed several insulin doses over the past few days because she has not been eating properly.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 11 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 21.3 mmol/L, which strongly supports DKA as the cause of this HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 24.5 mmHg (acceptable range 22.5-26.5). The measured PaCO2 is 24.5 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 137 - (99 + 11) = 27.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: DKA is driven by insulin deficiency, which leads to ketone production and a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Marked hyperglycaemia helps distinguish it from other ketoacidoses. Dehydration and electrolyte shifts often accompany the acid-base disturbance.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: DKA usually gives you a high anion gap acidosis with high glucose — but still check whether the CO₂ and bicarbonate behave the way they should.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 31-year-old man feels light-headed and weak after several days of frequent loose stools. He has been drinking very little and now looks dehydrated, with crampy abdominal discomfort.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 15 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss leads to a metabolic acidosis, reflected by a low bicarbonate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 30.5 mmHg (acceptable range 28.5-32.5). The measured PaCO2 is 30.7 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 141 - (114 + 15) = 12.0, which is normal. This makes a raised-gap metabolic acidosis less likely.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diarrhoea causes normal anion gap metabolic acidosis through direct bicarbonate loss from the gut. Chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality, creating a hyperchloraemic acidosis. The pattern may occur with volume depletion and low potassium.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A normal anion gap acidosis points away from hidden acids and toward bicarbonate loss — often from the gut.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 50-year-old man feels dizzy on standing and has had a dry mouth and increasing fatigue over the past few days. He recently had his water tablet increased for ankle swelling.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 35 mmol/L and has moved up with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic alkalosis. High HCO3 represents excess base in the blood, so it directly pushes the pH upward.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis. Diuretic use causes volume depletion and chloride loss, which maintains the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic alkalosis, the body compensates by hypoventilating to retain PaCO2. Using the metabolic alkalosis compensation rule, the expected PaCO2 is about 47.7 mmHg (acceptable range 44.7-50.7). The measured PaCO2 is 48.7 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diuretics can cause metabolic alkalosis through volume depletion and renal chloride loss. Chloride depletion helps the kidney retain bicarbonate, which maintains the alkalosis. Potassium is often low or low-normal.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 56F smoker presents with worsening breathlessness, chest tightness, and a productive cough over the past day. She is increasingly wheezy and drowsy by the time she arrives in ED.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 69.5 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis. CO2 behaves like an acid load: as PaCO2 rises, more carbonic acid is generated and the pH falls.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is consistent with an acute on chronic respiratory acidosis. Chronic CO2 retention leads to metabolic compensation, with an acute rise in the PaCO2 during exacerbation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by 4 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 35.8 mmol/L (acceptable range 33.8-37.8). The measured HCO3 is 32.2 mmol/L. The acute-only expected HCO3 is about 26.9 mmol/L (range 24.9-28.9). The measured HCO3 sits between the acute and stable chronic expected responses, supporting acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis without implying a precise combined target.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: An acute COPD exacerbation can worsen ventilation, causing PaCO₂ to rise further and pH to fall. The bicarbonate may already be elevated from chronic compensation, but it has not had time to fully match the acute change. This creates an acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis pattern.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: A 4-year-old boy is brought in by his parents after they applied a rubbing oil for a sore muscle and then realised it was oil of wintergreen. He has become sweaty, tachypnoeic, and vomited once, and his mother is very anxious that he may have swallowed some.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the classic respiratory alkalosis plus high anion gap metabolic acidosis pattern of salicylate toxicity

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 23.7 mmHg and has moved down while the pH has moved up, so the primary process is respiratory alkalosis. This is the respiratory side of ROME: PaCO2 and pH move in opposite directions, so losing CO2 raises the pH.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This is the classic salicylate pattern: respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In acute respiratory alkalosis, buffering lowers HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 should fall by 2 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 20.7 mmol/L (acceptable range 18.7-22.7). The measured HCO3 is 17.6 mmol/L. Because the measured HCO3 is below the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 141 - (103 + 17.6) = 20.4, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: No third process is identified. The compensation step suggested a second acid-base process, and the raised anion gap identifies this as HAGMA. Delta gap reasoning does not show an additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis on top of the HAGMA: the bicarbonate level is not clearly lower or higher than expected once the respiratory disorder and raised-gap metabolic acidosis are considered.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Salicylate toxicity classically causes both respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis. The two processes can partially offset each other, so the pH may look deceptively normal. The mixed pattern is the important clue.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Salicylates are sneaky: alkalosis and acidosis can happen at the same time, sometimes with a deceptively normal pH.

cases.title: Sepsis (respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 22-year-old man is brought in after a night of fever, rigors, and a worsening productive cough. He looks pale and confused, with rapid breathing and a low blood pressure.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits sepsis-related hyperventilation without an added metabolic process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In acute respiratory alkalosis, buffering lowers HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 should fall by 2 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 21.7 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.7-23.7). The measured HCO3 is 21.0 mmol/L. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate metabolic compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Sepsis can increase respiratory drive, causing hyperventilation and a low PaCO₂. This respiratory alkalosis may appear early, before a metabolic acidosis develops. A normal pH can still hide an important acid-base process.

questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 32 mmol/L and has moved up with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic alkalosis. High HCO3 represents excess base in the blood, so it directly pushes the pH upward.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis. Diuretic use causes volume depletion and chloride loss, which maintains the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic alkalosis, the body compensates by hypoventilating to retain PaCO2. Using the metabolic alkalosis compensation rule, the expected PaCO2 is about 45.6 mmHg (acceptable range 42.6-48.6). The measured PaCO2 is 46.4 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diuretics can cause metabolic alkalosis through volume depletion and renal chloride loss. Chloride depletion helps the kidney retain bicarbonate, which maintains the alkalosis. Potassium is often low or low-normal.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 27-year-old woman is brought in after a few days of vomiting, thirst, and worsening abdominal discomfort. She also reports a recent flu-like illness with poor oral intake and has been breathing deeply on arrival.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 10 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 32.5 mmol/L, which strongly supports DKA as the cause of this HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 23.0 mmHg (acceptable range 21.0-25.0). The measured PaCO2 is 23.9 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 136 - (96 + 10) = 30.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: DKA is driven by insulin deficiency, which leads to ketone production and a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Marked hyperglycaemia helps distinguish it from other ketoacidoses. Dehydration and electrolyte shifts often accompany the acid-base disturbance.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: DKA usually gives you a high anion gap acidosis with high glucose — but still check whether the CO₂ and bicarbonate behave the way they should.

cases.title: Massive PE physiology after maternal cardiac arrest
cases.learning_objective: Recognise severe mixed metabolic and respiratory acidosis with profound oxygenation failure after maternal PEA arrest, and integrate the clinical context with RV dilatation as massive PE physiology until proven otherwise.
cases.clinical_stem: ROSC is achieved in a young woman in her third trimester of pregnancy after a collapse and PEA arrest. She has been intubated, and is on a low-dose adrenaline infusion. An arterial blood gas was taken shortly after.

questions_flow.label: A-a gradient
questions_flow.prompt: Can the observed PaO₂ be explained by hypoventilation alone?
options: Yes - hypoxaemia is mostly explained by reduced alveolar oxygen from hypoventilation
No - there is impaired oxygen transfer from alveoli to arterial blood
There is no meaningful oxygenation abnormality
Cannot assess from the available data

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

questions_flow.label: Diagnosis
questions_flow.prompt: What is the most likely interpretation?
options: Massive PE physiology causing obstructive shock/PEA arrest
Post-arrest lactic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
Isolated ventilator hypoventilation causing respiratory acidosis
Amniotic fluid embolism as the only unifying diagnosis

answer_key.aa_gradient_mechanism: No - there is impaired oxygen transfer from alveoli to arterial blood
answer_key.final_diagnosis: Massive PE physiology causing obstructive shock/PEA arrest

explanation_blueprint.title: Oxygenation
explanation_blueprint.body: A PaO2 of 78 mmHg might look only mildly low in isolation, but this patient is on FiO2 1.0. This is severe oxygenation failure in a patient receiving maximal inspired oxygen.

explanation_blueprint.title: A-a gradient
explanation_blueprint.body: Using the alveolar gas equation, PAO₂ ≈ FiO₂ × (760 − 47) − PaCO₂/0.8. Here, PAO₂ is approximately 713 − 101 = 612 mmHg. With a measured PaO₂ of 78 mmHg, the A–a gradient is about 534 mmHg. This is markedly raised, meaning the low PaO₂ cannot be explained by hypoventilation alone; there is impaired oxygen transfer from alveoli to arterial blood.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 24.5 mmHg (acceptable range 22.5-26.5). The measured PaCO2 is 81.0 mmHg. Because the measured PaCO2 is above the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion gap
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 19 mmol/L, which is raised. This indicates a HAGMA, with the elevated lactate supporting lactic acidosis from cardiac arrest and shock.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional metabolic process
explanation_blueprint.body: The fall in bicarbonate is disproportionate to the rise in anion gap. This produces a low delta ratio and supports an NAGMA, likely related to hyperchloraemia in this peri-arrest/resuscitation context.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: In a post-ROSC pregnant patient, the combination of PEA arrest, refractory hypoxaemia on FiO₂ 1.0, severe mixed acidosis, and a dilated right ventricle is massive PE physiology until proven otherwise.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical context
explanation_blueprint.body: Pregnancy normally lowers PaCO₂ through increased minute ventilation. A PaCO₂ of 81 mmHg is therefore profoundly abnormal and should not be dismissed as a pregnancy-related change.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A true ABG should make you ask three questions: is oxygen getting in, is CO₂ getting out, and is perfusion failing? In this case, all three are abnormal — severe hypoxaemia on FiO₂ 1.0, profound CO₂ retention, and lactic acidosis.

cases.title: Simple normal anion gap metabolic acidosis
cases.clinical_stem: A 37-year-old woman presents with several days of watery diarrhoea, reduced oral intake, and worsening light-headedness on standing. She is tired, tachycardic, and clinically volume depleted.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise a simple bicarbonate-driven metabolic acidosis with a normal anion gap

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 18 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss leads to a metabolic acidosis, reflected by a low bicarbonate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 35.0 mmHg (acceptable range 33.0-37.0). The measured PaCO2 is 36.4 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 137 - (110 + 18) = 9.0, which is normal. This makes a raised-gap metabolic acidosis less likely.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Normal anion gap metabolic acidosis usually comes from bicarbonate loss or impaired renal acid excretion. As bicarbonate falls, chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality, creating a hyperchloraemic pattern. Gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss, such as diarrhoea, is a common cause.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: An 80-year-old woman comes in feeling weak and light-headed after several days of loose stools and poor oral intake. She also reports crampy lower abdominal discomfort and needing help to stand because of dehydration.

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 20 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss leads to a metabolic acidosis, reflected by a low bicarbonate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diarrhoea causes normal anion gap metabolic acidosis through direct bicarbonate loss from the gut. Chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality, creating a hyperchloraemic acidosis. The pattern may occur with volume depletion and low potassium.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A normal anion gap acidosis points away from hidden acids and toward bicarbonate loss — often from the gut.

cases.title: Toxic alcohol (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 36-year-old man is brought in after telling his family he drank something from the garage following an argument. He is confused, complaining of blurred vision and upper abdominal pain. The bottle beside him smells of antifreeze.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise toxic alcohol ingestion as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 9 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: In the appropriate clinical context, a HAGMA should prompt consideration of toxic alcohol ingestion.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 21.5 mmHg (acceptable range 19.5-23.5). The measured PaCO2 is 23.0 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 141 - (100 + 9) = 32.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Toxic alcohol ingestion can produce a severe raised anion gap metabolic acidosis as acidic metabolites accumulate. The acidosis may be out of proportion to the measured lactate. The key pattern is an unexplained high anion gap acidosis without another clear cause.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A severe unexplained high anion gap acidosis, especially with lactate that does not explain it, should make toxic alcohols ring alarm bells.

cases.title: Mixed HAGMA and metabolic alkalosis
cases.clinical_stem: A 24-year-old woman presents after several days of persistent vomiting, poor oral intake, and worsening abdominal discomfort. She looks dehydrated and weak, with ongoing nausea and a recent flu-like illness.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise a mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent metabolic alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 18 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is a mixed HAGMA with concurrent metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 35.0 mmHg (acceptable range 33.0-37.0). The measured PaCO2 is 34.5 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 136 - (91 + 18) = 27.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Delta gap reasoning compares the rise in anion gap with the fall in HCO3. With an anion gap of 27.0, a pure raised-gap metabolic acidosis would usually lower HCO3 to about 9.0 mmol/L. The measured HCO3 is 18 mmol/L, which is higher than expected. This means the bicarbonate has been relatively preserved, indicating an additional metabolic alkalosis. This pattern can occur when acid generation is combined with vomiting or diuretic effect.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern combines acid generation with a process that raises bicarbonate, such as vomiting or diuretic effect. The anion gap reveals the metabolic acidosis, while the higher-than-expected bicarbonate reveals the alkalosis. The key is recognising that both processes can be present at once.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap shows the acidosis; the bicarbonate tells you something is pushing back the other way.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with recurrent episodes of vomiting and inability to tolerate fluids. She is lethargic, thirsty, and clinically dehydrated, with no diarrhoea or fever.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 34 mmol/L and has moved up with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic alkalosis. High HCO3 represents excess base in the blood, so it directly pushes the pH upward.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from gastric losses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic alkalosis, the body compensates by hypoventilating to retain PaCO2. Using the metabolic alkalosis compensation rule, the expected PaCO2 is about 47.0 mmHg (acceptable range 44.0-50.0). The measured PaCO2 is 48.1 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 141 - (96 + 34) = 11.0, which is normal. This makes a raised-gap metabolic acidosis less likely.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Vomiting causes loss of gastric acid, raising bicarbonate and producing metabolic alkalosis. The body compensates by retaining CO₂ through hypoventilation. Ongoing chloride and volume depletion help keep the alkalosis going.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Vomiting removes stomach acid, but chloride depletion is what helps the alkalosis stick around.

final_diagnosis.title: Diagnosis
final_diagnosis.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from gastric losses.

compensation.title: Compensation

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: An 18-year-old man comes in feeling weak and light-headed after several days of frequent loose stools and poor oral intake. He also reports crampy abdominal discomfort and has noticed he is passing less urine.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.36, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 20 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss leads to a metabolic acidosis, reflected by a low bicarbonate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 38.0 mmHg (acceptable range 36.0-40.0). The measured PaCO2 is 36.8 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 137 - (108 + 20) = 9.0, which is normal. This makes a raised-gap metabolic acidosis less likely.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diarrhoea causes normal anion gap metabolic acidosis through direct bicarbonate loss from the gut. Chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality, creating a hyperchloraemic acidosis. The pattern may occur with volume depletion and low potassium.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A normal anion gap acidosis points away from hidden acids and toward bicarbonate loss — often from the gut.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 77-year-old man feels weak and light-headed after his water tablets were increased for ankle swelling. He also reports dry mouth and leg cramps over the past few days.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 32 mmol/L and has moved up with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic alkalosis. High HCO3 represents excess base in the blood, so it directly pushes the pH upward.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis. Diuretic use causes volume depletion and chloride loss, which maintains the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic alkalosis, the body compensates by hypoventilating to retain PaCO2. Using the metabolic alkalosis compensation rule, the expected PaCO2 is about 45.6 mmHg (acceptable range 42.6-48.6). The measured PaCO2 is 46.6 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diuretics can cause metabolic alkalosis through volume depletion and renal chloride loss. Chloride depletion helps the kidney retain bicarbonate, which maintains the alkalosis. Potassium is often low or low-normal.

cases.title: Respiratory acidosis with concurrent HAGMA
cases.clinical_stem: A 58-year-old man is brought in from the ward with increasing drowsiness and laboured breathing over the past few hours. He looks unwell, with a possible chest infection and reduced urine output on a background of chronic lung disease.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory acidosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 72.6 mmHg and has moved up while the pH has moved down, so the primary process is respiratory acidosis. CO2 behaves like an acid load: as PaCO2 rises, more carbonic acid is generated and the pH falls.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory acidosis with concurrent HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by 4 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 37.0 mmol/L (acceptable range 35.0-39.0). The measured HCO3 is 29.9 mmol/L. Because the measured HCO3 is below the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 142 - (92 + 29.9) = 20.1, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: No third process is identified. The compensation step suggested a second acid-base process, and the raised anion gap identifies this as HAGMA. Delta gap reasoning does not show an additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis on top of the HAGMA: the bicarbonate level is not clearly lower or higher than expected once the respiratory disorder and raised-gap metabolic acidosis are considered.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern combines impaired ventilation with a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. PaCO₂ retention pushes the pH down, while the metabolic acidosis adds a second acidifying process. The result is often a more severe acidaemia than either process alone.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high PaCO₂ plus a raised anion gap means two problems may be happening at once.

cases.title: Respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA
cases.clinical_stem: A 35-year-old woman arrives breathing rapidly, sweaty, and feeling increasingly unwell after a day of vomiting and vague abdominal pain. She is anxious, mildly confused, and says she has not been able to keep anything down.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory alkalosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.43, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 21.5 mmHg and has moved down while the pH has moved up, so the primary process is respiratory alkalosis. This is the respiratory side of ROME: PaCO2 and pH move in opposite directions, so losing CO2 raises the pH.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In acute respiratory alkalosis, buffering lowers HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 should fall by 2 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 20.3 mmol/L (acceptable range 18.3-22.3). The measured HCO3 is 13.9 mmol/L. Because the measured HCO3 is below the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 143 - (107 + 13.9) = 22.1, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: No third process is identified. The compensation step suggested a second acid-base process, and the raised anion gap identifies this as HAGMA. Delta gap reasoning does not show an additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis on top of the HAGMA: the bicarbonate level is not clearly lower or higher than expected once the respiratory disorder and raised-gap metabolic acidosis are considered.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern combines hyperventilation with a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. The respiratory alkalosis can lift the pH toward normal, masking the metabolic acidosis. Sepsis is one context where this mixed pattern can appear.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A very low PaCO₂ does not explain everything. Check the anion gap for the hidden metabolic acidosis.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the classic respiratory alkalosis plus high anion gap metabolic acidosis pattern of salicylate toxicity

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 29.9 mmHg and has moved down while the pH has moved up, so the primary process is respiratory alkalosis. This is the respiratory side of ROME: PaCO2 and pH move in opposite directions, so losing CO2 raises the pH.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This is the classic salicylate pattern: respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In acute respiratory alkalosis, buffering lowers HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the 1-2-4-5 rule for acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 should fall by 2 mmol/L for every 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2, giving an expected HCO3 of about 22.0 mmol/L (acceptable range 20.0-24.0). The measured HCO3 is 15.1 mmol/L. Because the measured HCO3 is below the expected range, this does not fit isolated compensation. A second acid-base process is likely present.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 143 - (103 + 15.1) = 24.9, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: No third process is identified. The compensation step suggested a second acid-base process, and the raised anion gap identifies this as HAGMA. Delta gap reasoning does not show an additional NAGMA or metabolic alkalosis on top of the HAGMA: the bicarbonate level is not clearly lower or higher than expected once the respiratory disorder and raised-gap metabolic acidosis are considered.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Salicylate toxicity classically causes both respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis. The two processes can partially offset each other, so the pH may look deceptively normal. The mixed pattern is the important clue.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Salicylates are sneaky: alkalosis and acidosis can happen at the same time, sometimes with a deceptively normal pH.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder

explanation_blueprint.body: Poor oral intake can increase ketone production and cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is usually milder than DKA because some insulin activity remains. The key clue is ketosis with normal or only mildly elevated glucose.

explanation_blueprint.body: Ketones without major hyperglycaemia are not automatically DKA. The story around the gas matters.

cases.title: Mixed HAGMA and metabolic alkalosis
cases.clinical_stem: A 72-year-old woman has had several days of poor intake and persistent vomiting after a brief bout of diarrhoea. She is now weak, lightheaded, and looks dehydrated.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic process
questions_flow.prompt: Is there an additional metabolic process?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise a mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent metabolic alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.35, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 23 mmol/L and has moved down with the pH, so the primary process is metabolic acidosis. Low HCO3 means there is less base available to buffer acid, which pushes the pH downward; this is the metabolic pattern in ROME.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is a mixed HAGMA with concurrent metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Acid-Base Processes
explanation_blueprint.body: The acid-base processes present are Metabolic acidosis and Metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 42.5 mmHg (acceptable range 40.5-44.5). The measured PaCO2 is 43.6 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 136 - (92 + 23) = 21.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Delta gap reasoning compares the rise in anion gap with the fall in HCO3. With an anion gap of 21.0, a pure raised-gap metabolic acidosis would usually lower HCO3 to about 15.0 mmol/L. The measured HCO3 is 23 mmol/L, which is higher than expected. This means the bicarbonate has been relatively preserved, indicating an additional metabolic alkalosis. This pattern can occur when acid generation is combined with vomiting or diuretic effect.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern combines acid generation with a process that raises bicarbonate, such as vomiting or diuretic effect. The anion gap reveals the metabolic acidosis, while the higher-than-expected bicarbonate reveals the alkalosis. The key is recognising that both processes can be present at once.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap shows the acidosis; the bicarbonate tells you something is pushing back the other way.

Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 42.5 mmHg (acceptable range 40.5-44.5). The measured PaCO2 is 43.6 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation. The anion gap is 136 - (92 + 23) = 21.0, which is raised. This supports a HAGMA pattern, driven by unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketones, or toxins. Delta gap reasoning compares the rise in anion gap with the fall in HCO3. With an anion gap of 21.0, a pure raised-gap metabolic acidosis would usually lower HCO3 to about 15.0 mmol/L. The measured HCO3 is 23 mmol/L, which is higher than expected. This means the bicarbonate has been relatively preserved, indicating an additional metabolic alkalosis. This pattern can occur when acid generation is combined with vomiting or diuretic effect. The overall pattern is a mixed HAGMA with concurrent metabolic alkalosis. This pattern combines acid generation with a process that raises bicarbonate, such as vomiting or diuretic effect. The anion gap reveals the metabolic acidosis, while the higher-than-expected bicarbonate reveals the alkalosis. The key is recognising that both processes can be present at once. The anion gap shows the acidosis; the bicarbonate tells you something is pushing back the other way.
